**Japan**[[a]](#cite_note-11) is an [island country](/wiki/Island_country "Island country") in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"). Located in the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") off the northeast coast of the [Asian mainland](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), it is bordered to the west by the [Sea of Japan](/wiki/Sea_of_Japan "Sea of Japan") and extends from the [Sea of Okhotsk](/wiki/Sea_of_Okhotsk "Sea of Okhotsk") in the north to the [East China Sea](/wiki/East_China_Sea "East China Sea") in the south. The [Japanese archipelago](/wiki/Japanese_archipelago "Japanese archipelago") consists of four major islands alongside [14,121 smaller islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Japan "List of islands of Japan"). Japan is divided into [47 administrative prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan") and [eight traditional regions](/wiki/List_of_regions_of_Japan "List of regions of Japan"), and around 75% of [its terrain](/wiki/Geography_of_Japan "Geography of Japan") is mountainous and heavily forested, concentrating [its agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Japan "Agriculture in Japan") and [highly urbanized](/wiki/Urbanization_by_country "Urbanization by country") population along its eastern [coastal plains](/wiki/Coastal_plain "Coastal plain"). With a population of almost 123 million as of 2026, it is the world's [11th most populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population"). [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") is [the country's capital](/wiki/Capital_of_Japan "Capital of Japan") and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Japan "List of cities in Japan").

The first known habitation of the [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") dates to the [Upper Paleolithic](/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic "Upper Paleolithic"), with the beginning of the [Japanese Paleolithic](/wiki/Japanese_Paleolithic "Japanese Paleolithic") dating to c. 36,000 BC. Between the 4th and 6th centuries, its kingdoms were united under [an emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") in [Nara](/wiki/Nara_(city) "Nara (city)") and later in [Heian-kyō](/wiki/Heian-ky%C5%8D "Heian-kyō"). From the 12th century, actual power was held by military aristocrats known as *[shōgun](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun "Shōgun")* and feudal lords called *[daimyō](/wiki/Daimy%C5%8D "Daimyō")*, enforced by warrior nobility named *[samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai")*.[[11]](#cite_note-12) After rule by the [Kamakura](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period") and [Ashikaga shogunates](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period") and [a century of warring states](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period"), Japan was unified in 1600 by the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate"), which implemented [an isolationist foreign policy](/wiki/Sakoku "Sakoku"). In 1853, [an American fleet](/wiki/Perry_Expedition "Perry Expedition") forced Japan to [open trade to the West](/wiki/Bakumatsu "Bakumatsu"), which led to the [end of the shogunate](/wiki/Boshin_War "Boshin War") and the [restoration of imperial power](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") in 1868.

The [Meiji period](/wiki/Meiji_period "Meiji period") saw Japan pursue rapid [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization"), [modernization](/wiki/Modernization "Modernization"), [militarism](/wiki/Japanese_militarism "Japanese militarism"), and [overseas colonization](/wiki/Japanese_colonial_empire "Japanese colonial empire"). The country [annexed Korea](/wiki/Korea_under_Japanese_rule "Korea under Japanese rule") in 1910, [invaded China](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") in 1937, and [attacked the U.S.](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor") and [European colonial powers](/wiki/European_colonial_powers "European colonial powers") in 1941, thus [entering World War II](/wiki/Japan_during_World_War_II "Japan during World War II") as an [Axis power](/wiki/Axis_power "Axis power"). After being defeated in the [Pacific War](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War") and suffering the U.S. [atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki"), Japan [surrendered in 1945](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan") and [came under Allied occupation](/wiki/Occupation_of_Japan "Occupation of Japan"). It underwent [rapid economic growth](/wiki/Japanese_economic_miracle "Japanese economic miracle") in the following decades and became one of the first [major non-NATO allies](/wiki/Major_non-NATO_allies "Major non-NATO allies") of the U.S. Since the collapse of the [Japanese asset price bubble](/wiki/Japanese_asset_price_bubble "Japanese asset price bubble") in the early 1990s, it has experienced a prolonged period of [economic stagnation](/wiki/Economic_stagnation "Economic stagnation") referred to as the [Lost Decades](/wiki/Lost_Decades "Lost Decades").

Japan is a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") with a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") legislature known as the [National Diet](/wiki/National_Diet "National Diet"). Widely considered a [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") and the only Asian member of the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7"), it maintains [one of the world's strongest militaries](/wiki/Japan_Self-Defense_Forces "Japan Self-Defense Forces") but has [constitutionally renounced its right to declare war](/wiki/Article_9_of_the_Japanese_Constitution "Article 9 of the Japanese Constitution"). A [developed country](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") with one of the world's [largest economies by nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)"), it is a global leader in the [automotive](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Japan "Automotive industry in Japan"), [electronics](/wiki/Electronics_industry_in_Japan "Electronics industry in Japan"), and [robotics](/wiki/Japanese_robotics "Japanese robotics") industries, in addition to making significant [contributions to science and technology](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Japan "Science and technology in Japan"). It has one of the world's [highest life expectancies](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy"), but is undergoing [a population decline](/wiki/Aging_of_Japan "Aging of Japan"). The [culture of Japan](/wiki/Culture_of_Japan "Culture of Japan") is well known around the world, particularly [its popular culture](/wiki/Japanese_popular_culture "Japanese popular culture") as expressed in [animation](/wiki/Anime "Anime"), [art](/wiki/Japanese_art "Japanese art"), [comics](/wiki/Manga "Manga"), [cuisine](/wiki/Japanese_cuisine "Japanese cuisine"), [fashion](/wiki/Japanese_clothing "Japanese clothing"), [films](/wiki/Cinema_of_Japan "Cinema of Japan"), [music](/wiki/Music_of_Japan "Music of Japan"), [television](/wiki/Television_in_Japan "Television in Japan"), [theatre](/wiki/Theatre_of_Japan "Theatre of Japan") and [video games](/wiki/Video_games_in_Japan "Video games in Japan").

## Etymology

Main article: [Names of Japan](/wiki/Names_of_Japan "Names of Japan")

The name for Japan in [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language") is written using the [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") 日本 and is pronounced *Nihon* or *Nippon*.[[12]](#cite_note-schreiber-13) Before 日本 was adopted in the early 8th century, the country was known in China as *[Wa](/wiki/Wa_(name_of_Japan) "Wa (name of Japan)")* (倭, changed in Japan around 757 to 和) and in Japan by the [endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym") *Yamato*.[[13]](#cite_note-carr-14) *Nippon*, the original [Sino-Japanese reading](/wiki/Sino-Japanese_reading "Sino-Japanese reading") of the characters, is favored for official uses, including on [Japanese banknotes](/wiki/Japanese_banknotes "Japanese banknotes") and postage stamps.[[12]](#cite_note-schreiber-13) *Nihon* is typically used in everyday speech and reflects shifts in [Japanese phonology](/wiki/Japanese_phonology "Japanese phonology") during the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period").[[13]](#cite_note-carr-14) The characters 日本 mean 'sun origin',[[12]](#cite_note-schreiber-13) which is the source of the popular Western [epithet](/wiki/Epithet "Epithet") "Land of the Rising Sun".[[14]](#cite_note-15)

The name "Japan" is based on [Min](/wiki/Min_Chinese "Min Chinese") or [Wu Chinese](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese") pronunciations of 日本 and was introduced to European languages through early trade.[[15]](#cite_note-Mancall-16) In the 13th century, [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo") recorded the [Early Mandarin](/wiki/Early_Mandarin "Early Mandarin") Chinese pronunciation of the characters 日本國 as *Cipangu*.[[16]](#cite_note-17) The old [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") name for Japan, *Japang* or *Japun*, was borrowed from a southern coastal Chinese dialect and encountered by [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") traders in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), who brought the word to Europe in the early 16th century.[[17]](#cite_note-18) The first version of the name in English appears in a book published in 1577, which spelled the name as *Giapan* in a translation of a 1565 Portuguese letter.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[15]](#cite_note-Mancall-16)

## History

Main article: [History of Japan](/wiki/History_of_Japan "History of Japan")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Japanese history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Japanese_history "Timeline of Japanese history").

### Prehistoric to classical history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Emperor_Jimmu.jpg/250px-Emperor_Jimmu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Jimmu.jpg)

[Legendary](/wiki/Legend "Legend") [Emperor Jimmu](/wiki/Emperor_Jimmu "Emperor Jimmu") (神武天皇, *Jinmu-tennō*)

Modern humans arrived in Japan around 38,000 years ago (~36,000 BC), marking the beginning of the [Japanese Paleolithic](/wiki/Japanese_Paleolithic "Japanese Paleolithic").[[19]](#cite_note-20) Around 14,500 BC (the start of the [Jōmon period](/wiki/J%C5%8Dmon_period "Jōmon period")), a [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic") to [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") semi-sedentary [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") culture characterized by [pit dwelling](/wiki/Pit_dwelling "Pit dwelling") and rudimentary agriculture emerged.[[20]](#cite_note-21) [Clay vessels](/wiki/J%C5%8Dmon_pottery "Jōmon pottery") from the period are among the oldest surviving examples of pottery.[[21]](#cite_note-22) The [Japonic](/wiki/Japonic "Japonic")-speaking [Yayoi people](/wiki/Yayoi_people "Yayoi people") later entered the archipelago from the Korean Peninsula,[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-Yayoi_culture-25) intermingling with the [Jōmon people](/wiki/J%C5%8Dmon_people "Jōmon people").[[24]](#cite_note-Yayoi_culture-25) The [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period") saw the introduction of innovative practices including [wet-rice farming](/wiki/Paddy_field "Paddy field"),[[25]](#cite_note-26) a new [style of pottery](/wiki/Yayoi_pottery "Yayoi pottery"),[[26]](#cite_note-27) and metallurgy from China and Korea.[[27]](#cite_note-28) According to legend, [Emperor Jimmu](/wiki/Emperor_Jimmu "Emperor Jimmu") (descendant of [Amaterasu](/wiki/Amaterasu "Amaterasu")) founded a kingdom in central Japan in 660 BC, beginning [a continuous imperial line](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan").[[28]](#cite_note-:1-29)

Japan first appears in written history in the Chinese *[Book of Han](/wiki/Book_of_Han "Book of Han")*, completed in 111 AD, where it is described as having a hundred small kingdoms. A century later, the *[Book of Wei](/wiki/Book_of_Wei "Book of Wei")* records that the kingdom of [Yamatai](/wiki/Yamatai "Yamatai") (which may refer to [Yamato](/wiki/Yamato_Kingship "Yamato Kingship")) unified most of these kingdoms.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[28]](#cite_note-:1-29) [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") was introduced to Japan from [Baekje](/wiki/Baekje "Baekje") (a Korean kingdom) in 552, but the development of [Japanese Buddhism](/wiki/Japanese_Buddhism "Japanese Buddhism") was primarily influenced by China.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Despite early resistance, Buddhism was promoted by the ruling class, including figures like [Prince Shōtoku](/wiki/Prince_Sh%C5%8Dtoku "Prince Shōtoku"), and gained widespread acceptance beginning in the [Asuka period](/wiki/Asuka_period "Asuka period") (592–710).[[31]](#cite_note-32)

In 645, the government led by [Prince Naka no Ōe](/wiki/Prince_Naka_no_%C5%8Ce "Prince Naka no Ōe") and [Fujiwara no Kamatari](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Kamatari "Fujiwara no Kamatari") devised and implemented the far-reaching [Taika Reforms](/wiki/Taika_Reform "Taika Reform"). The Reform began with land reform, based on Confucian ideas and [philosophies](/wiki/Chinese_philosophy "Chinese philosophy") from [China](/wiki/China "China").[[32]](#cite_note-Totman_2005-33) It nationalized all land in Japan, to be [distributed equally](/wiki/Equal-field_system "Equal-field system") among cultivators, and ordered the compilation of a household registry as the basis for a new system of taxation.[[33]](#cite_note-Sansom-34) The true aim of the reforms was to bring about greater centralization and to enhance the power of the imperial court, which was also based on the governmental structure of China. Envoys and students were dispatched to China to learn about Chinese writing, politics, art, and religion.[[32]](#cite_note-Totman_2005-33)

The [Jinshin War](/wiki/Jinshin_War "Jinshin War") of 672, a bloody conflict between [Prince Ōama](/wiki/Prince_%C5%8Cama "Prince Ōama") and his nephew [Prince Ōtomo](/wiki/Prince_%C5%8Ctomo "Prince Ōtomo"), became a major catalyst for further administrative reforms.[[34]](#cite_note-ritsuryo-35) These reforms culminated with the promulgation of the [Taihō Code](/wiki/Taih%C5%8D_Code "Taihō Code"), which consolidated existing statutes and established the structure of the central and subordinate local governments.[[33]](#cite_note-Sansom-34) These legal reforms created the *[ritsuryō](/wiki/Ritsury%C5%8D "Ritsuryō")* state, a system of Chinese-style centralized government that remained in place for half a millennium.[[34]](#cite_note-ritsuryo-35)

The [Nara period](/wiki/Nara_period "Nara period") (710–784) marked the emergence of a Japanese state centered on the Imperial Court in [Heijō-kyō](/wiki/Heij%C5%8D_Palace "Heijō Palace") (modern [Nara](/wiki/Nara,_Nara "Nara, Nara")). The period is characterized by the appearance of a nascent [literary culture](/wiki/Japanese_literature "Japanese literature") with the completion of the *[Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki")* (712) and *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")* (720), as well as the development of Buddhist-inspired artwork and [architecture](/wiki/Historic_Monuments_of_Ancient_Nara "Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara").[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-Courtiers-37) A [smallpox epidemic in 735–737](/wiki/735%E2%80%93737_Japanese_smallpox_epidemic "735–737 Japanese smallpox epidemic") is believed to have killed as much as one-third of Japan's population.[[36]](#cite_note-Courtiers-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38) In 784, [Emperor Kanmu](/wiki/Emperor_Kanmu "Emperor Kanmu") moved the capital, settling on [Heian-kyō](/wiki/Heian-ky%C5%8D "Heian-kyō") (modern-day [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto")) in 794.[[36]](#cite_note-Courtiers-37) This marked the beginning of the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") (794–1185), during which a distinctly indigenous Japanese culture emerged. [Murasaki Shikibu](/wiki/Murasaki_Shikibu "Murasaki Shikibu")'s *[The Tale of Genji](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Genji "The Tale of Genji")* and the lyrics of Japan's national anthem "[Kimigayo](/wiki/Kimigayo "Kimigayo")" were written during this time.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### Feudal era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Mokoshuraiekotoba.jpg/250px-Mokoshuraiekotoba.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mokoshuraiekotoba.jpg)

Japanese [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") boarding a Mongol vessel during the [Mongol invasions of Japan](/wiki/Mongol_invasions_of_Japan "Mongol invasions of Japan"), depicted in the *[Mōko Shūrai Ekotoba](/wiki/M%C5%8Dko_Sh%C5%ABrai_Ekotoba "Mōko Shūrai Ekotoba")*, 1293

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/The_Three_Unifiers_of_Japan.jpg/250px-The_Three_Unifiers_of_Japan.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Three_Unifiers_of_Japan.jpg)

Three unifiers of Japan. Left to right: [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga"), [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"), and [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu").

Japan's feudal era was characterized by the emergence and dominance of a ruling class of warriors, the [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai").[[39]](#cite_note-Leibo2015-40) In 1185, following the defeat of the [Taira clan](/wiki/Taira_clan "Taira clan") by the [Minamoto clan](/wiki/Minamoto_clan "Minamoto clan") in the [Genpei War](/wiki/Genpei_War "Genpei War"), samurai [Minamoto no Yoritomo](/wiki/Minamoto_no_Yoritomo "Minamoto no Yoritomo") established a [military government](/wiki/Military_government "Military government") at [Kamakura](/wiki/Kamakura "Kamakura").[[40]](#cite_note-41) After Yoritomo's death, the [Hōjō clan](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_clan "Hōjō clan") came to power as regents for the *shōgun*.[[36]](#cite_note-Courtiers-37) The [Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen") school of Buddhism was introduced from China in the [Kamakura period](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period") (1185–1333) and became popular among the samurai class.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

The [Kamakura shogunate](/wiki/Kamakura_shogunate "Kamakura shogunate") repelled [Mongol invasions](/wiki/Mongol_invasions_of_Japan "Mongol invasions of Japan") in 1274 and 1281 but [was eventually overthrown](/wiki/Kenmu_Restoration "Kenmu Restoration") by [Emperor Go-Daigo](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Daigo "Emperor Go-Daigo").[[36]](#cite_note-Courtiers-37) Go-Daigo was defeated by [Ashikaga Takauji](/wiki/Ashikaga_Takauji "Ashikaga Takauji") in 1336, beginning the [Muromachi period](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period") (1336–1573).[[42]](#cite_note-Shirane2012a-43) The succeeding [Ashikaga shogunate](/wiki/Ashikaga_shogunate "Ashikaga shogunate") failed to control the feudal warlords (*daimyō*) and [a civil war](/wiki/%C5%8Cnin_War "Ōnin War") began in 1467, opening the century-long [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period") ('Warring States').[[43]](#cite_note-44)

During the 16th century, Portuguese traders and [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") missionaries reached Japan for the first time, initiating direct commercial and cultural exchange between Japan and the West (see [Nanban trade](/wiki/Nanban_trade "Nanban trade") and [Nanban art](/wiki/Nanban_art "Nanban art")).[[36]](#cite_note-Courtiers-37)[[44]](#cite_note-45) [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") used European technology and firearms to conquer many other *daimyō*;[[45]](#cite_note-46) his consolidation of power began what was known as the [Azuchi–Momoyama period](/wiki/Azuchi%E2%80%93Momoyama_period "Azuchi–Momoyama period").[[46]](#cite_note-47) After [the death of Nobunaga](/wiki/Honn%C5%8D-ji_Incident "Honnō-ji Incident") in 1582, his successor, [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"), unified the nation in the early 1590s and launched [two unsuccessful invasions of Korea in 1592 and 1597](/wiki/Japanese_invasions_of_Korea_(1592%E2%80%9398) "Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–98)").[[36]](#cite_note-Courtiers-37)

[Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") served as regent for Hideyoshi's son [Toyotomi Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori") within the [Council of Five Elders](/wiki/Council_of_Five_Elders "Council of Five Elders") and used his position to gain political and military support.[[47]](#cite_note-Turnbull2011-48) When open war broke out, Ieyasu defeated rival clans in the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara") in 1600. He was appointed *shōgun* by [Emperor Go-Yōzei](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Y%C5%8Dzei "Emperor Go-Yōzei") in 1603 and established the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate") at [Edo](/wiki/Edo "Edo") (modern Tokyo).[[48]](#cite_note-Closed-49) The shogunate enacted measures including *[Buke shohatto](/wiki/Buke_shohatto "Buke shohatto")*, as a code of conduct to control the autonomous *daimyō*,[[49]](#cite_note-50) and in 1639 the isolationist *[sakoku](/wiki/Sakoku "Sakoku")* ('closed country') policy that spanned the two and a half centuries of tenuous political unity known as the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") (1603–1868).[[48]](#cite_note-Closed-49)[[50]](#cite_note-51) Modern Japan's economic growth began in this period, resulting in roads (*[Kaidō](/wiki/Kaid%C5%8D "Kaidō")*) and water transportation routes, as well as financial instruments such as [futures contracts](/wiki/Futures_contract "Futures contract"), banking and insurance of the [Osaka rice brokers](/wiki/Osaka_rice_brokers "Osaka rice brokers").[[51]](#cite_note-52) The study of Western sciences (*[rangaku](/wiki/Rangaku "Rangaku")*) continued through contact with the Dutch enclave in [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki").[[48]](#cite_note-Closed-49) The Edo period gave rise to *[kokugaku](/wiki/Kokugaku "Kokugaku")* ('national studies'), the study of Japan by the Japanese.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

### Modern era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Meiji_tenno1.jpg/250px-Meiji_tenno1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meiji_tenno1.jpg)

[Emperor Meiji](/wiki/Emperor_Meiji "Emperor Meiji") (明治天皇, *Meiji-tennō*); 1852–1912

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Japanese_Empire_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg/250px-Japanese_Empire_%28orthographic_projection%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Japanese_Empire_(orthographic_projection).svg)

The [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") and its influence, 1942

The [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") sent Commodore [Matthew C. Perry](/wiki/Matthew_C._Perry "Matthew C. Perry") to force the opening of Japan to the outside world. Arriving at [Uraga](/wiki/Uraga,_Kanagawa "Uraga, Kanagawa") with four "[Black Ships](/wiki/Black_Ships "Black Ships")" in July 1853, the [Perry Expedition](/wiki/Perry_Expedition "Perry Expedition") resulted in the March 1854 [Convention of Kanagawa](/wiki/Convention_of_Kanagawa "Convention of Kanagawa").[[48]](#cite_note-Closed-49) Subsequent similar treaties with other Western countries brought economic and political crises.[[48]](#cite_note-Closed-49) The resignation of the *shōgun* led to the [Boshin War](/wiki/Boshin_War "Boshin War") and the establishment of a [centralized state](/wiki/Abolition_of_the_han_system "Abolition of the han system") nominally unified under the emperor (the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration")).[[53]](#cite_note-54) Adopting Western political, judicial, and military institutions, the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Japan "Cabinet of Japan") organized the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_(Japan) "Privy Council (Japan)"), introduced the [Meiji Constitution](/wiki/Meiji_Constitution "Meiji Constitution") (November 29, 1890), and assembled the [Imperial Diet](/wiki/National_Diet "National Diet").[[54]](#cite_note-modernnation-55)

During the [Meiji period](/wiki/Meiji_period "Meiji period") (1868–1912), the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") emerged as the most developed state in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and as an industrialized world power that pursued military conflict to expand its sphere of influence.[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58) After victories in the [First Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/First_Sino-Japanese_War "First Sino-Japanese War") (1894–1895) and the [Russo-Japanese War](/wiki/Russo-Japanese_War "Russo-Japanese War") (1904–1905), Japan gained control of Taiwan, Korea and the southern half of [Sakhalin](/wiki/Sakhalin "Sakhalin"),[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[54]](#cite_note-modernnation-55) and annexed Korea in 1910.[[59]](#cite_note-60) The Japanese population doubled from 35 million in 1873 to 70 million by 1935, with a significant shift to urbanization.[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-excesses-62)

The early 20th century saw a period of [Taishō democracy](/wiki/Taish%C5%8D_period "Taishō period") (1912–1926) overshadowed by increasing [expansionism](/wiki/Expansionism "Expansionism") and [militarization](/wiki/Japanese_militarism "Japanese militarism").[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-ramesh-64) [World War I](/wiki/Japan_during_World_War_I "Japan during World War I") allowed Japan, which joined the side of the victorious [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"), to capture [German possessions](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire") in the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific "Pacific") and China in [1920](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles").[[63]](#cite_note-ramesh-64) The 1920s saw a political shift towards [statism](/wiki/Statism_in_Sh%C5%8Dwa_Japan "Statism in Shōwa Japan"), a period of lawlessness following the 1923 [Great Tokyo Earthquake](/wiki/Great_Tokyo_Earthquake "Great Tokyo Earthquake"), the passing of [laws against political dissent](/wiki/Peace_Preservation_Law "Peace Preservation Law"), and a series of [attempted coups](/wiki/May_15_Incident "May 15 Incident").[[61]](#cite_note-excesses-62)[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)

This process accelerated in the 1930s, spawning several radical nationalist groups that shared a hostility to liberal democracy and a dedication to expansion in Asia.[[66]](#cite_note-67) In 1931, Japan [invaded China and occupied Manchuria](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_Manchuria "Japanese invasion of Manchuria"), which led to the establishment of [puppet state](/wiki/Puppet_state "Puppet state") of [Manchukuo](/wiki/Manchukuo "Manchukuo") in 1932; following [international condemnation of the occupation](/wiki/Lytton_Report "Lytton Report"), it resigned from the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") in 1933.[[67]](#cite_note-Inc1945-68) In 1936, Japan signed the [Anti-Comintern Pact](/wiki/Anti-Comintern_Pact "Anti-Comintern Pact") with [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"); the 1940 [Tripartite Pact](/wiki/Tripartite_Pact "Tripartite Pact") made it one of the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers").[[61]](#cite_note-excesses-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Mamoru_Shigemitsu_signs_the_Instrument_of_Surrender%2C_officially_ending_the_Second_World_War.jpg/250px-Mamoru_Shigemitsu_signs_the_Instrument_of_Surrender%2C_officially_ending_the_Second_World_War.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mamoru_Shigemitsu_signs_the_Instrument_of_Surrender,_officially_ending_the_Second_World_War.jpg)

On September 2, 1945, Japan [surrendered to the Allies](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan").

The Empire of Japan invaded other parts of China in 1937, precipitating the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") (1937–1945).[[68]](#cite_note-Paine2012-69) In 1940, the Empire [invaded French Indochina](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_French_Indochina "Japanese invasion of French Indochina"), after which the United States placed an oil embargo on Japan.[[61]](#cite_note-excesses-62)[[69]](#cite_note-70) On December 7–8, 1941, Japanese forces carried out surprise [attacks on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attacks_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attacks on Pearl Harbor"), as well as on British forces in [Malaya](/wiki/Battle_of_Malaya "Battle of Malaya"), [Singapore](/wiki/Bombing_of_Singapore_(1941) "Bombing of Singapore (1941)"), and [Hong Kong](/wiki/Battle_of_Hong_Kong "Battle of Hong Kong"), among others, beginning [World War II in the Pacific](/wiki/World_War_II_in_the_Pacific "World War II in the Pacific").[[70]](#cite_note-71) Throughout areas occupied by Japan during the war, [numerous abuses](/wiki/Japanese_war_crimes "Japanese war crimes") were committed against local inhabitants. Many women were forced into [sexual slavery](/wiki/Comfort_women "Comfort women").[[71]](#cite_note-72)

After [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") victories during the next four years, which culminated in the [Soviet invasion of Manchuria](/wiki/Soviet_invasion_of_Manchuria "Soviet invasion of Manchuria") and the [atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki") in 1945, Japan agreed to [an unconditional surrender](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan").[[72]](#cite_note-73) The war cost Japan millions of lives and many of its [conquered territories](/wiki/Territorial_conquests_of_the_Empire_of_Japan "Territorial conquests of the Empire of Japan"), including *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* parts of Japan such as [Korea](/wiki/Korea_under_Japanese_rule "Korea under Japanese rule"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan_under_Japanese_rule "Taiwan under Japanese rule"), [Karafuto](/wiki/Karafuto "Karafuto"), and the [Kurils](/wiki/Kurils "Kurils").[[61]](#cite_note-excesses-62) The Allies, led by the United States, repatriated millions of [Japanese settlers](/wiki/Japanese_diaspora "Japanese diaspora") from their former colonies and military camps throughout Asia, largely eliminating the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") and its influence over the territories it conquered.[[73]](#cite_note-74)[[74]](#cite_note-phoenix-75) The Allies convened the [International Military Tribunal for the Far East](/wiki/International_Military_Tribunal_for_the_Far_East "International Military Tribunal for the Far East") to prosecute Japanese leaders except [the Emperor](/wiki/Hirohito "Hirohito")[[75]](#cite_note-76) for [Japanese war crimes](/wiki/Japanese_war_crimes "Japanese war crimes").[[74]](#cite_note-phoenix-75)

In 1947, Japan adopted [a new constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan") emphasizing liberal democratic practices.[[74]](#cite_note-phoenix-75) The [Allied occupation](/wiki/Occupation_of_Japan "Occupation of Japan") ended with the [Treaty of San Francisco](/wiki/Treaty_of_San_Francisco "Treaty of San Francisco") in 1952,[[76]](#cite_note-77) and Japan was granted membership in the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") in 1956.[[74]](#cite_note-phoenix-75) [A period of record growth](/wiki/Japanese_economic_miracle "Japanese economic miracle") propelled Japan to become the world's [second-largest economy at that time](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_largest_historical_GDP "List of countries by largest historical GDP");[[74]](#cite_note-phoenix-75) this ended in the mid-1990s after the popping of [an asset price bubble](/wiki/Japanese_asset_price_bubble "Japanese asset price bubble"), beginning the "[Lost Decade](/wiki/Lost_Decade_(Japan) "Lost Decade (Japan)")" characterized by economic stagnation and low inflation.[[77]](#cite_note-78) In 2011, Japan suffered one of the largest earthquakes in its recorded history—the [Tōhoku earthquake](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake "Tōhoku earthquake")—triggering the [Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_nuclear_disaster "Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster").[[78]](#cite_note-nytimes-tsunami-79) On May 1, 2019, after the historic [abdication of Emperor Akihito](/wiki/Abdication_of_Emperor_Akihito "Abdication of Emperor Akihito"), his son [Naruhito](/wiki/Naruhito "Naruhito") became Emperor, beginning the [Reiwa era](/wiki/Reiwa_era "Reiwa era").[[79]](#cite_note-80)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of Japan](/wiki/Geography_of_Japan "Geography of Japan") and [Geology of Japan](/wiki/Geology_of_Japan "Geology of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Japan_topo_en.jpg/330px-Japan_topo_en.jpg)](/wiki/File:Japan_topo_en.jpg)

A topographic map of Japan

Japan comprises [14,125 islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Japan "List of islands of Japan") extending along the Pacific coast of Asia.[[80]](#cite_note-81) It stretches over 3000 km (1900 mi) northeast–southwest from the [Sea of Okhotsk](/wiki/Sea_of_Okhotsk "Sea of Okhotsk") to the [East China Sea](/wiki/East_China_Sea "East China Sea").[[81]](#cite_note-water-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83) The country's five main islands, from north to south, are [Hokkaido](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido"), [Honshu](/wiki/Honshu "Honshu"), [Shikoku](/wiki/Shikoku "Shikoku"), [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu") and [Okinawa](/wiki/Okinawa_Island "Okinawa Island").[[83]](#cite_note-84) The [Ryukyu Islands](/wiki/Ryukyu_Islands "Ryukyu Islands"), which include Okinawa, are a chain to the south of Kyushu. The [Nanpō Islands](/wiki/Nanp%C5%8D_Islands "Nanpō Islands") are south and east of the main islands of Japan. Together they are often known as the [Japanese archipelago](/wiki/Japanese_archipelago "Japanese archipelago").[[84]](#cite_note-85) As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit), Japan's territory is 377,975.24 km2 (145,937.06 sq mi).[[4]](#cite_note-area-2019-4) Japan has the [sixth-longest coastline](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_length_of_coastline "List of countries by length of coastline") in the world at 29,751 km (18,486 mi). Because of its far-flung outlying islands, [Japan's exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone_of_Japan "Exclusive economic zone of Japan") is the [eighth-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_length_of_exclusive_economic_zones "List of countries by length of exclusive economic zones") in the world, covering 4,470,000 km2 (1,730,000 sq mi).[[85]](#cite_note-cia-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87)

The Japanese archipelago is 67% [forests](/wiki/Forestry_in_Japan "Forestry in Japan") and 14% [agricultural](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Japan "Agriculture in Japan").[[87]](#cite_note-88) The primarily rugged and mountainous terrain is restricted for habitation.[[88]](#cite_note-89) Thus the habitable zones, mainly in the coastal areas, have very high population densities: Japan is the [40th most densely populated country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_density "List of countries by population density") even without considering that local concentration.[[89]](#cite_note-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91) Honshu has the highest population density at 450 persons/km2 (1200/sq mi) as of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit), while Hokkaido has the lowest density of 64.5 persons/km2 as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[91]](#cite_note-JPCensus-92) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit), approximately 0.5% of Japan's total area is [reclaimed land](/wiki/Reclaimed_land "Reclaimed land") (*umetatechi*).[[92]](#cite_note-93) [Lake Biwa](/wiki/Lake_Biwa "Lake Biwa") is an [ancient lake](/wiki/Ancient_lake "Ancient lake") and the country's largest freshwater lake.[[93]](#cite_note-Tabata2016-94)

Japan is substantially prone to [earthquakes](/wiki/Earthquakes_in_Japan "Earthquakes in Japan"), [tsunami](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami") and [volcanic eruptions](/wiki/Volcanic_eruptions "Volcanic eruptions") because of its location along the Pacific Ring of Fire.[[94]](#cite_note-95) It has the [17th highest natural disaster risk](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_natural_disaster_risk "List of countries by natural disaster risk") as measured in the 2016 World Risk Index.[[95]](#cite_note-wri-96) Japan has 111 active volcanoes.[[96]](#cite_note-97) Destructive earthquakes, often resulting in tsunami, occur several times each century;[[97]](#cite_note-98) the [1923 Tokyo earthquake](/wiki/1923_Tokyo_earthquake "1923 Tokyo earthquake") killed over 140,000 people.[[98]](#cite_note-99) More recent major quakes are the 1995 [Great Hanshin earthquake](/wiki/Great_Hanshin_earthquake "Great Hanshin earthquake") and the [2011 Tōhoku earthquake](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake "2011 Tōhoku earthquake"), which triggered a large tsunami.[[78]](#cite_note-nytimes-tsunami-79)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Japan](/wiki/Climate_of_Japan "Climate of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Series-N700a-Mt.Fuji.jpg/250px-Series-N700a-Mt.Fuji.jpg)](/wiki/File:Series-N700a-Mt.Fuji.jpg)

[Mount Fuji](/wiki/Mount_Fuji "Mount Fuji") and the [Shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen")

The climate of Japan is predominantly temperate but varies greatly from north to south. The northernmost region, Hokkaido, has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") with long, cold winters and very warm to cool summers. [Precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation "Precipitation") is not heavy, but the islands usually develop deep snowbanks in the winter.[[99]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-100)

In the [Sea of Japan](/wiki/Sea_of_Japan "Sea of Japan") region on Honshu's west coast, northwest winter winds bring heavy snowfall during winter. In the summer, the region sometimes experiences extremely hot temperatures because of the [Foehn](/wiki/Foehn "Foehn").[[100]](#cite_note-101) The [Central Highland](/wiki/Central_Highland_(Japan) "Central Highland (Japan)") has a typical inland humid continental climate, with large temperature differences between summer and winter. The mountains of the [Chūgoku](/wiki/Ch%C5%ABgoku "Chūgoku") and Shikoku regions shelter the [Seto Inland Sea](/wiki/Seto_Inland_Sea "Seto Inland Sea") from seasonal winds, bringing mild weather year-round.[[99]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-100)

The Pacific coast features a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") that experiences milder winters with occasional snowfall and hot, humid summers because of the southeast seasonal wind. The Ryukyu and Nanpō Islands have a [subtropical climate](/wiki/Subtropical_climate "Subtropical climate"), with warm winters and hot summers. Precipitation is very heavy, especially during the rainy season.[[99]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-100) The main [rainy season](/wiki/East_Asian_rainy_season "East Asian rainy season") begins in early May in Okinawa, and the rain front gradually moves north. In late summer and early autumn, [typhoons](/wiki/Typhoon "Typhoon") often bring heavy rain.[[101]](#cite_note-102) According to the Environment Ministry, heavy rainfall and increasing temperatures have caused problems in the agricultural industry and elsewhere.[[102]](#cite_note-climatechange-103) The highest temperature ever measured in Japan, 41.8 °C (107.2 °F), was recorded on August 5, 2025.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Wildlife of Japan](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Japan "Wildlife of Japan")

Japan has nine forest [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregions_in_Japan "Ecoregions in Japan") which reflect the climate and geography of the islands. They range from [subtropical moist broadleaf forests](/wiki/Subtropical_moist_broadleaf_forests "Subtropical moist broadleaf forests") in the Ryūkyū and [Bonin Islands](/wiki/Bonin_Islands "Bonin Islands"), to [temperate broadleaf and mixed forests](/wiki/Temperate_broadleaf_and_mixed_forests "Temperate broadleaf and mixed forests") in the mild climate regions of the main islands, to [temperate coniferous forests](/wiki/Temperate_coniferous_forest "Temperate coniferous forest") in the cold, winter portions of the northern islands.[[104]](#cite_note-105) Japan has over 90,000 species of wildlife as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit),[[105]](#cite_note-Sakurai2019-106) including the [brown bear](/wiki/Brown_bear "Brown bear"), the [Japanese macaque](/wiki/Japanese_macaque "Japanese macaque"), the [Japanese raccoon dog](/wiki/Japanese_raccoon_dog "Japanese raccoon dog"), the [small Japanese field mouse](/wiki/Small_Japanese_field_mouse "Small Japanese field mouse"), and the [Japanese giant salamander](/wiki/Japanese_giant_salamander "Japanese giant salamander").[[106]](#cite_note-107) There are 53 [Ramsar wetland sites](/wiki/Ramsar_sites_in_Japan "Ramsar sites in Japan") in Japan.[[107]](#cite_note-108) [Five sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites_in_Japan "World Heritage Sites in Japan") have been inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List for their outstanding natural value.[[108]](#cite_note-109)

### Environment

Main articles: [Environmental issues in Japan](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Japan "Environmental issues in Japan") and [Climate change in Japan](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Japan "Climate change in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Kongobuji_Koyasan07n3200.jpg/250px-Kongobuji_Koyasan07n3200.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kongobuji_Koyasan07n3200.jpg)

Autumn [maple leaves](/wiki/Maple_tree "Maple tree") (*[momiji](/wiki/Momiji "Momiji")*) at [Kongōbu-ji](/wiki/Kong%C5%8Dbu-ji "Kongōbu-ji") on [Mount Kōya](/wiki/Mount_K%C5%8Dya "Mount Kōya"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site")

In the period of rapid economic growth after World War II, environmental policies were downplayed by the government and industrial corporations; as a result, [environmental pollution](/wiki/Four_Big_Pollution_Diseases_of_Japan "Four Big Pollution Diseases of Japan") was widespread in the 1950s and 1960s. Responding to rising concerns, the government introduced environmental protection laws in 1970.[[109]](#cite_note-110) The [oil crisis in 1973](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") also encouraged the efficient use of energy because of Japan's lack of natural resources.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

Japan ranks 20th in the 2018 [Environmental Performance Index](/wiki/Environmental_Performance_Index "Environmental Performance Index"), which measures a country's commitment to environmental sustainability.[[111]](#cite_note-112) Japan is the world's [fifth-largest emitter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_carbon_dioxide_emissions "List of countries by carbon dioxide emissions") of [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide").[[102]](#cite_note-climatechange-103) As the host and signatory of the 1997 [Kyoto Protocol](/wiki/Kyoto_Protocol "Kyoto Protocol"), Japan is under treaty obligation to reduce its carbon dioxide emissions and to take other steps to curb climate change.[[112]](#cite_note-113) In 2020, the government of Japan announced a target of [carbon-neutrality](/wiki/Carbon-neutral "Carbon-neutral") by 2050.[[113]](#cite_note-114) Environmental issues include urban air pollution ([NOx](/wiki/NOx "NOx"), suspended [particulate matter](/wiki/Particulate_matter "Particulate matter"), and [toxics](/wiki/Toxics "Toxics")), [waste management](/wiki/Waste_management "Waste management"), water [eutrophication](/wiki/Eutrophication "Eutrophication"), [nature conservation](/wiki/Nature_conservation "Nature conservation"), [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Japan "Climate change in Japan"), chemical management and international co-operation for conservation.[[114]](#cite_note-115)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Government of Japan](/wiki/Government_of_Japan "Government of Japan") and [Politics of Japan](/wiki/Politics_of_Japan "Politics of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Emperor_Naruhito_20250611_%2854582524056%2C_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Emperor_Naruhito_20250611_%2854582524056%2C_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Naruhito_20250611_(54582524056,_cropped).jpg)

[Naruhito](/wiki/Naruhito "Naruhito")  
[Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Official_portrait_of_Sanae_Takaichi%2C_Prime_Minister_of_Japan_%28HD%29.jpg/250px-Official_portrait_of_Sanae_Takaichi%2C_Prime_Minister_of_Japan_%28HD%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Official_portrait_of_Sanae_Takaichi,_Prime_Minister_of_Japan_(HD).jpg)

[Sanae Takaichi](/wiki/Sanae_Takaichi "Sanae Takaichi")  
[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Japan "Prime Minister of Japan")

Japan is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") and [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") in which the power of the [emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Japan "Emperor of Japan") (*Tennō*) is limited to a [ceremonial role](/wiki/Ceremonial_head_of_state "Ceremonial head of state").[[115]](#cite_note-euparl-116) Executive power is instead wielded by the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Japan "Prime Minister of Japan") and the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Japan "Cabinet of Japan"), whose sovereignty is vested in the Japanese people.[[116]](#cite_note-Constitution-117) [Naruhito](/wiki/Naruhito "Naruhito") is the Emperor of Japan, having succeeded his father [Akihito](/wiki/Akihito "Akihito") upon his accession to the [Chrysanthemum Throne](/wiki/Chrysanthemum_Throne "Chrysanthemum Throne") in 2019.[[115]](#cite_note-euparl-116)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/National_Diet_Building_02.jpg/250px-National_Diet_Building_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Diet_Building_02.jpg)

The [National Diet Building](/wiki/National_Diet_Building "National Diet Building")

Japan's legislative organ is the [National Diet](/wiki/National_Diet "National Diet"), a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") parliament.[[115]](#cite_note-euparl-116) It consists of a lower [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_(Japan) "House of Representatives (Japan)") with 465 seats, elected by popular vote every four years or when dissolved, and an upper [House of Councillors](/wiki/House_of_Councillors "House of Councillors") with 248 seats, whose popularly elected members serve six-year terms.[[117]](#cite_note-sec-118) There is [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") for adults over 18 years of age,[[118]](#cite_note-119) with a [secret ballot](/wiki/Secret_ballot "Secret ballot") for all elected offices.[[116]](#cite_note-Constitution-117) The prime minister as the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") has the power to appoint and dismiss [Ministers of State](/wiki/Ministers_of_State "Ministers of State"), and is [appointed](/wiki/Imperial_Investiture "Imperial Investiture") by the emperor after being designated from among the members of the Diet.[[117]](#cite_note-sec-118) [Sanae Takaichi](/wiki/Sanae_Takaichi "Sanae Takaichi") is Japan's prime minister; she took office after winning the [2025 Liberal Democratic Party leadership election](/wiki/2025_Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan)_presidential_election "2025 Liberal Democratic Party (Japan) presidential election") and securing a [confidence and supply](/wiki/Confidence_and_supply "Confidence and supply") agreement with [Ishin](/wiki/Japan_Innovation_Party "Japan Innovation Party").[[119]](#cite_note-120) The broadly conservative [Liberal Democratic Party](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") has been the [dominant party](/wiki/Dominant-party_system "Dominant-party system") in the country since the 1950s, often called the [1955 System](/wiki/1955_System "1955 System").[[120]](#cite_note-121)

Historically influenced by [Chinese law](/wiki/Chinese_law "Chinese law"), the Japanese legal system developed independently during the Edo period through texts such as *[Kujikata Osadamegaki](/wiki/Kujikata_Osadamegaki "Kujikata Osadamegaki")*.[[121]](#cite_note-dean-122) Since the late 19th century, [the judicial system](/wiki/Judicial_system_of_Japan "Judicial system of Japan") has been largely based on the [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") of Europe, notably Germany. In 1896, Japan established a [civil code](/wiki/Civil_code "Civil code") based on the German [Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch](/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerliches_Gesetzbuch "Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch"), which remains in effect with post–World War II modifications.[[122]](#cite_note-123) The [Constitution of Japan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan"), adopted in 1947, is the oldest unamended constitution in the world.[[123]](#cite_note-anomalous-constitution-124) Statutory law originates in the legislature, and the constitution requires that the emperor promulgate legislation passed by the Diet without giving him the power to oppose legislation. The main body of Japanese statutory law is called the [Six Codes](/wiki/Six_Codes "Six Codes").[[121]](#cite_note-dean-122) Japan's court system is divided into four basic tiers: the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Japan "Supreme Court of Japan") and three levels of lower courts.[[124]](#cite_note-125)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Administrative divisions of Japan](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Japan "Administrative divisions of Japan") and [Prefectures of Japan](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan")
