### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Japan](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Japan "Foreign relations of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Participation_of_Ursula_von_der_Leyen%2C_President_of_the_European_Commission%2C_to_the_G7_Summit.jpg/250px-Participation_of_Ursula_von_der_Leyen%2C_President_of_the_European_Commission%2C_to_the_G7_Summit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Participation_of_Ursula_von_der_Leyen,_President_of_the_European_Commission,_to_the_G7_Summit.jpg)

Japan is a member of both the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7") and the [G20](/wiki/G20 "G20").

A member state of the United Nations since 1956, Japan is one of the [G4 countries](/wiki/G4_nations "G4 nations") seeking reform of the [Security Council](/wiki/Security_Council "Security Council").[[126]](#cite_note-127) Japan is a member of the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7"), [APEC](/wiki/APEC "APEC"), and "[ASEAN Plus Three](/wiki/ASEAN_Free_Trade_Area#ASEAN_Plus_Three "ASEAN Free Trade Area")", and is a participant in the [East Asia Summit](/wiki/East_Asia_Summit "East Asia Summit").[[127]](#cite_note-terada-128) It is the world's [fifth-largest donor](/wiki/List_of_development_aid_sovereign_state_donors "List of development aid sovereign state donors") of [official development assistance](/wiki/Official_Development_Assistance_(Japan) "Official Development Assistance (Japan)"), donating US$9.2 billion in 2014.[[128]](#cite_note-129) In 2024, Japan had the [fourth-largest diplomatic network](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_diplomatic_missions "List of countries by number of diplomatic missions") in the world.[[129]](#cite_note-130) Japan is widely considered to be a [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") due to its [economic power](/wiki/Economic_power "Economic power") and political, cultural, and military influence.[[130]](#cite_note-Milena_Steriob-131)[[131]](#cite_note-PaulWirtz2004-132)

Japan has close economic and military [relations with the United States](/wiki/Japan%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Japan–United States relations"), with which it maintains [a security alliance](/wiki/Treaty_of_Mutual_Cooperation_and_Security_between_the_United_States_and_Japan "Treaty of Mutual Cooperation and Security between the United States and Japan").[[132]](#cite_note-us-relations-133) The United States is a major market for Japanese exports and a major source of Japanese imports, and is committed to defending the country, with military bases in Japan.[[132]](#cite_note-us-relations-133) In 2016, Japan announced the Free and Open Indo-Pacific vision, which frames its regional policies.[[133]](#cite_note-134)[[134]](#cite_note-135) Japan is also a member of the [Quadrilateral Security Dialogue](/wiki/Quadrilateral_Security_Dialogue "Quadrilateral Security Dialogue") ("the Quad"), a multilateral security dialogue reformed in 2017 aiming to limit Chinese influence in the [Indo-Pacific](/wiki/Indo-Pacific "Indo-Pacific") region, along with the United States, Australia, and India.[[135]](#cite_note-136)[[136]](#cite_note-137)

Japan is engaged in several territorial disputes with its neighbors. It contests Russia's control of the [Southern Kuril Islands](/wiki/Kuril_Islands_dispute "Kuril Islands dispute"), which were occupied by the Soviet Union in 1945.[[137]](#cite_note-138) South Korea's control of the [Liancourt Rocks](/wiki/Liancourt_Rocks "Liancourt Rocks") is acknowledged but not accepted as they are claimed by Japan.[[138]](#cite_note-takeshima-139) Japan's relations with China are marked by both deep economic ties as well as tensions over Taiwan, security, and the [Senkaku Islands](/wiki/Senkaku_Islands "Senkaku Islands").[[139]](#cite_note-140)[[140]](#cite_note-141)

### Military

Main article: [Japan Self-Defense Forces](/wiki/Japan_Self-Defense_Forces "Japan Self-Defense Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/US_Navy_051115-N-8492C-125_The_Japan_Maritime_Self-Defense_Force_%28JMSDF%29_destroyer_JDS_Kongou_%28DDG_173%29_sails_in_formation_with_other_JMSDF_ships_and_ships_assigned_to_the_USS_Kitty_Hawk_Carrier_Strike_Group.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_051115-N-8492C-125_The_Japan_Maritime_Self-Defense_Force_(JMSDF)_destroyer_JDS_Kongou_(DDG_173)_sails_in_formation_with_other_JMSDF_ships_and_ships_assigned_to_the_USS_Kitty_Hawk_Carrier_Strike_Group.jpg)

[*Kongō*-class destroyer](/wiki/Kong%C5%8D-class_destroyer "Kongō-class destroyer") of the [Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force](/wiki/Japan_Maritime_Self-Defense_Force "Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force")

Japan is the third highest-ranked Asian country in the 2024 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index").[[141]](#cite_note-142) It spent 1.4% of its total GDP on [its defense budget](/wiki/Military_budget_of_Japan "Military budget of Japan") and maintained the [tenth-largest military budget](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures") in the world in 2024.[[142]](#cite_note-SIPRI-2020-143) The country's military, the Japan Self-Defense Forces (JSDF), is restricted by [Article 9 of the Japanese Constitution](/wiki/Article_9_of_the_Japanese_Constitution "Article 9 of the Japanese Constitution"), which renounces Japan's right to declare war or use military force in international disputes.[[143]](#cite_note-144) The military is governed by the [Ministry of Defense](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defense_(Japan) "Ministry of Defense (Japan)"), and primarily consists of the [Japan Ground Self-Defense Force](/wiki/Japan_Ground_Self-Defense_Force "Japan Ground Self-Defense Force"), the [Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force](/wiki/Japan_Maritime_Self-Defense_Force "Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force"), and the [Japan Air Self-Defense Force](/wiki/Japan_Air_Self-Defense_Force "Japan Air Self-Defense Force"). The [deployment of troops to Iraq](/wiki/Japanese_Iraq_Reconstruction_and_Support_Group "Japanese Iraq Reconstruction and Support Group") and Afghanistan marked the first overseas use of Japan's military since World War II.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

The [Government of Japan](/wiki/Government_of_Japan "Government of Japan") has been making changes to its security policy which include the establishment of the [National Security Council](/wiki/National_Security_Council_(Japan) "National Security Council (Japan)"), the adoption of the National Security Strategy, and the development of the National Defense Program Guidelines.[[145]](#cite_note-Japan's_Security_Policy-146) In 2015, the Japanese parliament passed the [Legislation for Peace and Security](/wiki/Legislation_for_Peace_and_Security "Legislation for Peace and Security"), allowing the JSDF to participate in foreign conflicts in an "existential crisis situation".[[146]](#cite_note-kuro2-147) In December 2022, Prime Minister [Fumio Kishida](/wiki/Fumio_Kishida "Fumio Kishida") instructed the government to increase spending by 65% until 2027.[[147]](#cite_note-148) Recent tensions, particularly with North Korea and China, have reignited the debate over the status of the JSDF and its relation to Japanese society.[[148]](#cite_note-149)[[149]](#cite_note-150)

### Law enforcement

Main articles: [Law enforcement in Japan](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Japan "Law enforcement in Japan") and [Law of Japan](/wiki/Law_of_Japan "Law of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/%E8%AD%A6%E8%A6%96%E5%BA%81_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-%E8%AD%A6%E8%A6%96%E5%BA%81_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E8%AD%A6%E8%A6%96%E5%BA%81_-_panoramio.jpg)

The headquarters of the [Tokyo Metropolitan Police Department](/wiki/Tokyo_Metropolitan_Police_Department "Tokyo Metropolitan Police Department")

Domestic security in Japan is provided mainly by the [prefectural police departments](/wiki/Prefectural_police_department "Prefectural police department"), under the oversight of the [National Police Agency](/wiki/National_Police_Agency_(Japan) "National Police Agency (Japan)").[[150]](#cite_note-151) As the central coordinating body for the Prefectural Police Departments, the National Police Agency is administered by the [National Public Safety Commission](/wiki/National_Public_Safety_Commission_(Japan) "National Public Safety Commission (Japan)").[[151]](#cite_note-NPA1977-2-152) The [Special Assault Team](/wiki/Special_Assault_Team "Special Assault Team") comprises national-level [counter-terrorism](/wiki/Counter-terrorism "Counter-terrorism") tactical units that cooperate with territorial-level [Anti-Firearms Squads and Counter-NBC Terrorism Squads](/wiki/Riot_Police_Unit#Specialist_squads "Riot Police Unit").[[152]](#cite_note-WP-153) The [Japan Coast Guard](/wiki/Japan_Coast_Guard "Japan Coast Guard") guards territorial waters surrounding Japan and uses surveillance and control countermeasures against smuggling, marine [environmental crime](/wiki/Environmental_crime "Environmental crime"), poaching, piracy, spy ships, unauthorized foreign fishing vessels, and illegal immigration.[[153]](#cite_note-154)

The [Firearm and Sword Possession Control Law](/wiki/Firearm_and_Sword_Possession_Control_Law "Firearm and Sword Possession Control Law") strictly regulates the civilian ownership of guns, swords, and other weaponry.[[154]](#cite_note-155)[[155]](#cite_note-156) According to the [United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime](/wiki/United_Nations_Office_on_Drugs_and_Crime "United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime"), among the member states of the UN that report statistics as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit), the incidence rates of violent crimes such as murder, abduction, sexual violence, and robbery are very low in Japan.[[156]](#cite_note-157)[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159)[[159]](#cite_note-160)

### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in Japan](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Japan "Human rights in Japan")

Japanese society traditionally places a strong emphasis on [collective harmony](/wiki/Wa_(Japanese_culture) "Wa (Japanese culture)") and [conformity](/wiki/Collectivism "Collectivism"), which has led to the suppression of [individual rights](/wiki/Individual_rights "Individual rights").[[160]](#cite_note-h191-161) [Japan's constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan") prohibits racial and religious discrimination,[[161]](#cite_note-162)[[162]](#cite_note-163) and the country is a signatory to numerous international human rights treaties.[[163]](#cite_note-q456-164) However, it lacks any laws against discrimination based on race, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation, or gender identity and does not have a national human rights institution.[[164]](#cite_note-p620-165)

Japan has faced criticism for [its gender inequality](/wiki/Gender_inequality_in_Japan "Gender inequality in Japan"),[[165]](#cite_note-p015-166) [not allowing same-sex marriages](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Japan "Same-sex marriage in Japan"),[[166]](#cite_note-h253-167) use of [racial profiling](/wiki/Racial_profiling "Racial profiling") by police,[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169) and [allowing capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_Japan "Capital punishment in Japan").[[169]](#cite_note-170) Other human rights issues include the treatment of marginalized groups, such as [ethnic minorities](/wiki/Racism_in_Japan "Racism in Japan"),[[170]](#cite_note-r037-171) [refugees and asylum seekers](/wiki/Immigration_to_Japan#Refugees_and_asylum_seekers "Immigration to Japan").[[171]](#cite_note-v702-172)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Japan](/wiki/Economy_of_Japan "Economy of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Nakanoshima_Skyscrapers_in_201504_001.jpg/250px-Nakanoshima_Skyscrapers_in_201504_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nakanoshima_Skyscrapers_in_201504_001.jpg)

Skyscrapers in [Nakanoshima](/wiki/Nakanoshima "Nakanoshima"), Osaka; a major [financial center](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center") in Japan

Japan has the world's [fourth-largest economy by nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)"), after that of the United States, China and Germany; and the [fifth-largest by PPP-adjusted GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)").[[172]](#cite_note-173) As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit), [Japan's labor force](/wiki/Labor_market_of_Japan "Labor market of Japan") is the world's [tenth-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_labour_force "List of countries by labour force"), consisting of over 69.2 million workers.[[85]](#cite_note-cia-86) As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit), Japan has a [low unemployment rate](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_unemployment_rate "List of countries by unemployment rate") of around 2.6%.[[173]](#cite_note-174) [Its poverty rate](/wiki/Poverty_in_Japan "Poverty in Japan") is the second highest among the G7 countries,[[174]](#cite_note-175) and exceeds 15.7% of the population.[[175]](#cite_note-176) Japan has the highest ratio of public debt to GDP among advanced economies,[[176]](#cite_note-177) with [a national debt](/wiki/National_debt_of_Japan "National debt of Japan") estimated at 237% relative to GDP as of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[177]](#cite_note-178) The [Japanese yen](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen") is the world's third-largest [reserve currency](/wiki/Reserve_currency "Reserve currency") after the US dollar and the euro.[[178]](#cite_note-179)

In 2024, Japan was the world's [eight-largest exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports")[[179]](#cite_note-180) and [sixth-largest importer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports").[[180]](#cite_note-181) Its exports amounted to 22.8% of its total GDP in 2024.[[181]](#cite_note-182) In 2025, [Japan's main export markets](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_trading_partners_of_Japan "List of the largest trading partners of Japan") were China (22.8%, including Hong Kong) and the United States (18.5%).[[182]](#cite_note-STATJETRO-183) [Its main exports](/wiki/List_of_exports_of_Japan "List of exports of Japan") are motor vehicles, iron and steel products, semiconductors, and auto parts.[[85]](#cite_note-cia-86) Japan's main import markets in 2025 were China (23.8%) and the United States (11.4%).[[182]](#cite_note-STATJETRO-183) Japan's main imports are machinery and equipment, fossil fuels, foodstuffs, chemicals, and raw materials.[[182]](#cite_note-STATJETRO-183)

The Japanese variant of capitalism has many distinct features: *[keiretsu](/wiki/Keiretsu "Keiretsu")* enterprises are influential, and [lifetime employment](/wiki/Lifetime_employment "Lifetime employment") and seniority-based career advancement are common in the [Japanese work environment](/wiki/Japanese_work_environment "Japanese work environment").[[183]](#cite_note-oecd2008-184)[[184]](#cite_note-185) Japan has a large [cooperative](/wiki/Cooperative "Cooperative") sector, with three of the world's ten largest cooperatives, including the largest [consumer cooperative](/wiki/Consumer_cooperative "Consumer cooperative") and the largest [agricultural cooperative](/wiki/Agricultural_cooperative "Agricultural cooperative") as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[185]](#cite_note-186) It [ranks highly](/wiki/International_rankings_of_Japan#Economy "International rankings of Japan") for [competitiveness](/wiki/Competition_(companies) "Competition (companies)") and [economic freedom](/wiki/Economic_freedom "Economic freedom"). The country attracted 36.9 million international tourists in 2024,[[186]](#cite_note-187) and was [ranked eleventh](/wiki/World_Tourism_rankings "World Tourism rankings") in the world in 2019 for [inbound tourism](/wiki/Inbound_tourism_(Japan) "Inbound tourism (Japan)").[[187]](#cite_note-WTOB-188) The 2024 *[Travel and Tourism Competitiveness Report](/wiki/Travel_and_Tourism_Competitiveness_Report "Travel and Tourism Competitiveness Report")* ranked Japan third in the world.[[188]](#cite_note-189) Its international tourism receipts in 2019 amounted to $46.1 billion.[[187]](#cite_note-WTOB-188)

### Agriculture and fishery

Main article: [Agriculture, forestry, and fishing in Japan](/wiki/Agriculture,_forestry,_and_fishing_in_Japan "Agriculture, forestry, and fishing in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Rice_Paddies_In_Aizu%2C_Japan.JPG/250px-Rice_Paddies_In_Aizu%2C_Japan.JPG)](/wiki/File:Rice_Paddies_In_Aizu,_Japan.JPG)

A [rice paddy](/wiki/Rice_paddy "Rice paddy") in [Aizu](/wiki/Aizu "Aizu"), [Fukushima Prefecture](/wiki/Fukushima_Prefecture "Fukushima Prefecture")

The Japanese agricultural sector accounts for about 1.2% of the country's total GDP as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[117]](#cite_note-sec-118) Only 11.2% of Japan's land is suitable for cultivation.[[189]](#cite_note-190) Because of this lack of arable land, a system of [terraces](/wiki/Terrace_(earthworks) "Terrace (earthworks)") is used to farm in small areas.[[190]](#cite_note-Urbanites_Help_Sustain_Japan's_Historic_Rice_Paddy_Terraces-191) This results in one of the world's highest levels of crop yields per unit area, with an agricultural self-sufficiency rate of about 50% as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[191]](#cite_note-192) Japan's small agricultural sector is highly subsidized and [protected](/wiki/Agricultural_Protectionism_in_Japan "Agricultural Protectionism in Japan").[[192]](#cite_note-193) There has been a growing concern about farming as farmers are aging with a difficult time finding successors.[[193]](#cite_note-194)

Japan ranked seventh in the world in [tonnage of fish caught](/wiki/Fishing_industry_by_country "Fishing industry by country") and captured 3,167,610 metric tons of fish in 2016, down from an annual average of 4,000,000 tons over the previous decade.[[194]](#cite_note-195) Japan maintains one of the world's largest fishing fleets and accounts for nearly 15% of the global catch,[[85]](#cite_note-cia-86) prompting critiques that Japan's fishing is leading to depletion in fish stocks such as [tuna](/wiki/Tuna "Tuna").[[195]](#cite_note-196) Japan has sparked controversy by supporting commercial [whaling](/wiki/Whaling_in_Japan "Whaling in Japan").[[196]](#cite_note-197)

### Industry and services

Main articles: [Manufacturing in Japan](/wiki/Manufacturing_in_Japan "Manufacturing in Japan"), [Trade and services in Japan](/wiki/Trade_and_services_in_Japan "Trade and services in Japan"), [Electronics industry in Japan](/wiki/Electronics_industry_in_Japan "Electronics industry in Japan"), and [Automotive industry in Japan](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Japan "Automotive industry in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/2018_Nissan_GT-R_Premium_in_Super_Silver%2C_Front_Right%2C_10-11-2022.jpg/250px-2018_Nissan_GT-R_Premium_in_Super_Silver%2C_Front_Right%2C_10-11-2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_Nissan_GT-R_Premium_in_Super_Silver,_Front_Right,_10-11-2022.jpg)

The [Nissan GT-R](/wiki/Nissan_GT-R "Nissan GT-R"), a [sports car](/wiki/Sports_car "Sports car") manufactured by [Nissan](/wiki/Nissan "Nissan"). Japan is the [third-largest producer of motor vehicles](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_motor_vehicle_production "List of countries by motor vehicle production") in the world.[[197]](#cite_note-:0-198)

Japan has a large industrial capacity and is home to some of the "largest and most technologically advanced producers of motor vehicles, [machine tools](/wiki/Machine_tool "Machine tool"), steel and nonferrous metals, ships, [chemical substances](/wiki/Chemical_substance "Chemical substance"), textiles, and [processed foods](/wiki/Processed_foods "Processed foods")".[[85]](#cite_note-cia-86) Japan's industrial sector makes up approximately 27.5% of its GDP.[[85]](#cite_note-cia-86) The country's manufacturing output is the [fourth highest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_manufacturing_output "List of countries by manufacturing output") in the world as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[198]](#cite_note-199)

Japan is in the top three globally for both automobile production[[197]](#cite_note-:0-198) and export,[[199]](#cite_note-200)[[200]](#cite_note-201) and is home to [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota"), the world's [largest automobile company by production](/wiki/List_of_manufacturers_by_motor_vehicle_production "List of manufacturers by motor vehicle production"). The Japanese shipbuilding industry faces increasing competition from its East Asian neighbors, South Korea and China; a 2020 government initiative identified this sector as a target for increasing exports.[[201]](#cite_note-202)

Once considered the strongest in the world, the [Japanese consumer electronics industry](/wiki/Japanese_consumer_electronics_industry "Japanese consumer electronics industry") is in a state of decline as regional competition arises in neighboring East Asian countries such as South Korea and China.[[202]](#cite_note-203) However, [Japan's video game sector](/wiki/Video_games_in_Japan "Video games in Japan") remains a major industry; in 2014, Japan's consumer video game market grossed $9.6 billion, with $5.8 billion coming from mobile gaming.[[203]](#cite_note-204) By 2015, Japan had become the world's [fourth-largest](/wiki/List_of_video_games_markets_by_country "List of video games markets by country") [PC game](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") market by revenue, behind [China](/wiki/Video_games_in_China "Video games in China"), the [United States](/wiki/Video_games_in_the_United_States "Video games in the United States"), and [South Korea](/wiki/Video_games_in_South_Korea "Video games in South Korea").[[204]](#cite_note-205)

Japan's service sector accounts for about 69.8% of its total economic output as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[205]](#cite_note-206) [Banking](/wiki/Banking_in_Japan "Banking in Japan"), retail, [transportation](/wiki/Transportation_in_Japan "Transportation in Japan"), and [telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Japan "Telecommunications in Japan") are all major industries, with companies such as Toyota, [Honda](/wiki/Honda "Honda"), [Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Motor_Company "Yamaha Motor Company"), [Mitsubishi UFJ](/wiki/Mitsubishi_UFJ "Mitsubishi UFJ"), [NTT](/wiki/Nippon_Telegraph_and_Telephone "Nippon Telegraph and Telephone"), [Aeon](/wiki/Aeon_(company) "Aeon (company)"), [SoftBank](/wiki/SoftBank "SoftBank"), [Hitachi](/wiki/Hitachi "Hitachi"), [Mitsui](/wiki/Mitsui "Mitsui"), and [Itochu](/wiki/Itochu "Itochu") listed as among the largest in the world.[[206]](#cite_note-Fortune_Global_500-207)[[207]](#cite_note-208)

### Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Japan](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Japan "Science and technology in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Kibo_PM_and_ELM-PS.jpg/250px-Kibo_PM_and_ELM-PS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kibo_PM_and_ELM-PS.jpg)

The [Japanese Experiment Module](/wiki/Japanese_Experiment_Module "Japanese Experiment Module") (*Kibō*) at the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station")

Relative to gross domestic product, Japan's [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development_in_Japan "Research and development in Japan") budget is the [sixth or seventh highest](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_research_and_development_spending "List of sovereign states by research and development spending") in the world,[[208]](#cite_note-209) with 907,400 researchers sharing a 22-trillion-yen research and development budget as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[209]](#cite_note-210) Japan has the second highest number of researchers in science and technology per capita in the world with 14 per 1000 employees.[[210]](#cite_note-211) The country has produced twenty-two [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") in either physics, chemistry or medicine,[[211]](#cite_note-212) and three [Fields medalists](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal").[[212]](#cite_note-213)

Japan is a leading [robotics](/wiki/Japanese_robotics "Japanese robotics") producer, supplying 38% of the world's 2024 total,[[213]](#cite_note-214) down from 55% in 2017.[[214]](#cite_note-215)

The [Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency](/wiki/Japan_Aerospace_Exploration_Agency "Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency") is Japan's national [space agency](/wiki/Space_agency "Space agency"); it conducts space, planetary, and aviation research, and leads development of rockets and satellites.[[215]](#cite_note-216) It is a participant in the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station"): the [Japanese Experiment Module](/wiki/Japanese_Experiment_Module "Japanese Experiment Module") (*Kibō*) was added to the station during [Space Shuttle](/wiki/Space_Shuttle "Space Shuttle") assembly flights in 2008.[[216]](#cite_note-217) The [space probe](/wiki/Space_probe "Space probe") *[Akatsuki](/wiki/Akatsuki_(spacecraft) "Akatsuki (spacecraft)")* was launched in 2010 and achieved orbit around Venus in 2015.[[217]](#cite_note-218) Japan's plans in [space exploration](/wiki/Space_exploration "Space exploration") include building a [Moon base](/wiki/Colonization_of_the_Moon "Colonization of the Moon") and landing astronauts by 2030.[[218]](#cite_note-219) In 2007, it launched lunar explorer [SELENE](/wiki/SELENE "SELENE") (Selenological and Engineering Explorer) from [Tanegashima Space Center](/wiki/Tanegashima_Space_Center "Tanegashima Space Center"). The largest lunar mission since the [Apollo program](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program"), its purpose was to gather data on the [Moon's origin and evolution](/wiki/Moon#Formation "Moon"). The explorer entered a lunar orbit on October 4, 2007,[[219]](#cite_note-220)[[220]](#cite_note-221) and was deliberately crashed into the Moon on June 11, 2009.[[221]](#cite_note-222)

## Infrastructure

### Transportation

Main article: [Transport in Japan](/wiki/Transport_in_Japan "Transport in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Japan_Airlines%2C_Boeing_787-9_JA861J_NRT_%2819455285040%29.jpg/250px-Japan_Airlines%2C_Boeing_787-9_JA861J_NRT_%2819455285040%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Japan_Airlines,_Boeing_787-9_JA861J_NRT_(19455285040).jpg)

[Japan Airlines](/wiki/Japan_Airlines "Japan Airlines"), the [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") of Japan

Japan has invested heavily in transportation infrastructure since the 1990s.[[222]](#cite_note-223) The country has approximately 1,200,000 kilometers (750,000 miles) of roads made up of 1,000,000 kilometers (620,000 miles) of city, town, and village roads, 130,000 kilometers (81,000 miles) of prefectural roads, 54,736 kilometers (34,011 miles) of general national highways, and 7641 kilometers (4748 miles) of [national expressways](/wiki/Expressways_of_Japan "Expressways of Japan") as of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[223]](#cite_note-224)

Since privatization in 1987,[[224]](#cite_note-225) [dozens of Japanese railway companies](/wiki/List_of_railway_companies_in_Japan "List of railway companies in Japan") compete in regional and local passenger transportation markets; major companies include seven [JR](/wiki/Japan_Railways_Group "Japan Railways Group") enterprises, [Kintetsu](/wiki/Kintetsu_Railway "Kintetsu Railway"), [Seibu Railway](/wiki/Seibu_Railway "Seibu Railway") and [Keio Corporation](/wiki/Keio_Corporation "Keio Corporation"). The high-speed [Shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen") (bullet trains) that connect major cities are known for their safety and punctuality.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

There are [280 airports in Japan](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Japan "List of airports in Japan") as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[85]](#cite_note-cia-86) The largest domestic airport, [Haneda Airport](/wiki/Haneda_Airport "Haneda Airport") in Tokyo, was Asia's [second-busiest airport](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by passenger traffic") in 2019.[[226]](#cite_note-227) The Keihin and Hanshin superport hubs are among the largest in the world, at 7.98 and 5.22 million [TEU](/wiki/Twenty-foot_equivalent_unit "Twenty-foot equivalent unit") respectively as of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[227]](#cite_note-228)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in Japan](/wiki/Energy_in_Japan "Energy in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Setokazenooka-park01.jpg/250px-Setokazenooka-park01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Setokazenooka-park01.jpg)

Part of the [Seto Hill Windfarm](/wiki/Seto_Windhill "Seto Windhill")

As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit), 37.1% of energy in Japan is produced from petroleum, 25.1% from coal, 22.4% from [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), 3.5% from [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower") and 2.8% from [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_Japan "Nuclear power in Japan"), among other sources. Nuclear power was down from 11.2% in 2010.[[228]](#cite_note-229) By May 2012 all of [the country's nuclear power plants](/wiki/List_of_nuclear_power_plants_in_Japan "List of nuclear power plants in Japan") had been taken offline because of ongoing public opposition following the [Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster](/wiki/Fukushima_Daiichi_nuclear_disaster "Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster") in March 2011, though government officials continued to try to sway public opinion in favor of returning at least some to service.[[229]](#cite_note-230) The [Sendai Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Sendai_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Sendai Nuclear Power Plant") restarted in 2015,[[230]](#cite_note-231) and since then several other nuclear power plants have been restarted.[[231]](#cite_note-232) Japan lacks significant domestic reserves and has a heavy dependence on [imported energy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_oil_imports "List of countries by oil imports").[[232]](#cite_note-233) The country has therefore aimed to diversify its sources and maintain high levels of energy efficiency.[[233]](#cite_note-234)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Japan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Japan "Demographics of Japan")

See also: [Japanese people](/wiki/Japanese_people "Japanese people") and [Ethnic groups of Japan](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_of_Japan "Ethnic groups of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Tokyo_from_the_top_of_the_SkyTree.JPG/330px-Tokyo_from_the_top_of_the_SkyTree.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_from_the_top_of_the_SkyTree.JPG)

View of Tokyo from the top of the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree "Tokyo Skytree"). The [Greater Tokyo Area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area") is ranked as the [most populous metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_by_population "List of metropolitan areas by population") in the world.

Japan has a population of nearly 123 million, of whom almost 119 million are Japanese nationals; foreign residents make up the remainder, estimated at 3,743,000 as of September 2025.[[234]](#cite_note-235) Japan is one of the world's [fastest aging countries](/wiki/Aging_of_Japan "Aging of Japan") and has the highest proportion of [elderly citizens](/wiki/Elderly_people_in_Japan "Elderly people in Japan") of any country, comprising [one-third of its total population](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_age_structure "List of countries by age structure");[[235]](#cite_note-EUAgeing-236) this is the result of a [post–World War II baby boom](/wiki/Post%E2%80%93World_War_II_baby_boom "Post–World War II baby boom"), which was followed by an increase in life expectancy and a decrease in [birth rates](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate").[[236]](#cite_note-237)

Japan has a [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") of 1.2, which is below the [replacement rate](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility "Sub-replacement fertility") of 2.1, and is among [the world's lowest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_total_fertility_rate "List of countries by total fertility rate"):[[237]](#cite_note-238) it has a [median age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of 48.4, the [highest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_median_age "List of countries by median age").[[238]](#cite_note-IMFAgeing-239) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit), over 29.3% of the population is over 65, or more than one in four out of the Japanese population.[[239]](#cite_note-November_2020_population_estimate-240) As a growing number of younger Japanese are not marrying or remaining childless,[[240]](#cite_note-Walia-241)[[241]](#cite_note-242) Japan's population is expected to drop to around 88 million by 2065.[[235]](#cite_note-EUAgeing-236)

The changes in demographic structure have created several social issues, particularly a decline in the workforce population and an increase in the cost of social security benefits.[[240]](#cite_note-Walia-241) The Government of Japan projects that there will be almost one elderly person for each person of working age by 2060.[[238]](#cite_note-IMFAgeing-239) [Immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_Japan "Immigration to Japan") and birth incentives are sometimes suggested as a solution to provide younger workers to support the nation's aging population.[[242]](#cite_note-243)[[243]](#cite_note-244) On April 1, 2019, Japan's revised immigration law was enacted, protecting the rights of foreign workers to help reduce labor shortages in certain sectors.[[244]](#cite_note-245)

In 2023, 92% of the Japanese population lived in cities.[[245]](#cite_note-246) The capital city, Tokyo, has a population of 13.9 million (2022).[[246]](#cite_note-toukei-metro-247) It is part of the [Greater Tokyo Area](/wiki/Greater_Tokyo_Area "Greater Tokyo Area"), the [biggest metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_by_population "List of metropolitan areas by population") in the world with 37.4 million people (2024).[[247]](#cite_note-248) Japan is an ethnically and culturally [homogeneous society](/wiki/List_of_countries_ranked_by_ethnic_and_cultural_diversity_level "List of countries ranked by ethnic and cultural diversity level"),[[248]](#cite_note-249) with the [Japanese people](/wiki/Japanese_people "Japanese people") forming 97.4% of the country's population.[[249]](#cite_note-250) Minority ethnic groups in the country include the indigenous [Ainu](/wiki/Ainu_people "Ainu people") and [Ryukyuan people](/wiki/Ryukyuan_people "Ryukyuan people").[[250]](#cite_note-251) [Zainichi Koreans](/wiki/Zainichi_Koreans "Zainichi Koreans"),[[251]](#cite_note-252) [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_Japan "Chinese people in Japan"),[[252]](#cite_note-253) [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipinos_in_Japan "Filipinos in Japan"),[[253]](#cite_note-254)  [Brazilians mostly of Japanese descent](/wiki/Japanese_Brazilian "Japanese Brazilian"),[[254]](#cite_note-255) and  [Peruvians mostly of Japanese descent](/wiki/Japanese_Peruvian "Japanese Peruvian") are also among Japan's small minority groups.[[255]](#cite_note-256) *[Burakumin](/wiki/Burakumin "Burakumin")* make up a social minority group.[[256]](#cite_note-257)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Japan](/wiki/Languages_of_Japan "Languages of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/%E8%B1%9A%E9%AA%A8%E3%82%89%E3%83%BC%E3%82%81%E3%82%93_%E5%8D%9A%E5%A4%9A%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E_%E3%81%84%E3%82%89%E3%81%A3%E3%81%97%E3%82%83%E3%81%84_2010_%285023366778%29.jpg/250px-%E8%B1%9A%E9%AA%A8%E3%82%89%E3%83%BC%E3%82%81%E3%82%93_%E5%8D%9A%E5%A4%9A%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E_%E3%81%84%E3%82%89%E3%81%A3%E3%81%97%E3%82%83%E3%81%84_2010_%285023366778%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E8%B1%9A%E9%AA%A8%E3%82%89%E3%83%BC%E3%82%81%E3%82%93_%E5%8D%9A%E5%A4%9A%E5%A4%A9%E7%A5%9E_%E3%81%84%E3%82%89%E3%81%A3%E3%81%97%E3%82%83%E3%81%84_2010_(5023366778).jpg)

[Kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") and [hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana") signs

The [Japanese language](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language") is Japan's *de facto* national language and the primary written and spoken language of most people in the country.[[257]](#cite_note-LangPolicy-258) [Japanese writing](/wiki/Japanese_writing "Japanese writing") uses [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") ([Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character")) and two sets of [kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana") ([syllabaries](/wiki/Syllabaries "Syllabaries") based on [cursive script](/wiki/Cursive_script_(East_Asia) "Cursive script (East Asia)") and [radicals](/wiki/Radical_(Chinese_characters) "Radical (Chinese characters)") used by kanji), as well as the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") and [Arabic numerals](/wiki/Arabic_numerals "Arabic numerals").[[258]](#cite_note-259) English has taken a major role in Japan as a business and international [link language](/wiki/Link_language "Link language"), and is a compulsory subject at the junior and senior high school levels.[[259]](#cite_note-260) [Japanese Sign Language](/wiki/Japanese_Sign_Language "Japanese Sign Language") is the primary [sign language](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language") used in Japan and has gained some official recognition, but its usage has been historically hindered by discriminatory policies and a lack of educational support.[[257]](#cite_note-LangPolicy-258)

Besides Japanese, the [Ryukyuan languages](/wiki/Ryukyuan_languages "Ryukyuan languages") ([Amami](/wiki/Amami_%C5%8Cshima_language "Amami Ōshima language"), [Kunigami](/wiki/Kunigami_language "Kunigami language"), [Okinawan](/wiki/Okinawan_language "Okinawan language"), [Miyako](/wiki/Miyakoan_language "Miyakoan language"), [Yaeyama](/wiki/Yaeyama_language "Yaeyama language"), [Yonaguni](/wiki/Yonaguni_language "Yonaguni language")), part of the [Japonic language family](/wiki/Japonic_language_family "Japonic language family"), are spoken in the Ryukyu Islands chain.[[260]](#cite_note-261) Few children learn these languages,[[261]](#cite_note-262) but local governments have sought to increase awareness of the traditional languages.[[262]](#cite_note-263) The [Ainu language](/wiki/Ainu_language "Ainu language"), which is a [language isolate](/wiki/Language_isolate "Language isolate"), is [moribund](/wiki/Moribund_language "Moribund language"), with only a few native speakers remaining as of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[263]](#cite_note-264) Additionally, a number of other languages are taught and used by ethnic minorities, immigrant communities, and a growing number of foreign-language students, such as [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language") (including a distinct [Zainichi Korean dialect](/wiki/Zainichi_Korean_language "Zainichi Korean language")), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language").[[257]](#cite_note-LangPolicy-258)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Japan](/wiki/Religion_in_Japan "Religion in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Itsukushima_Gate.jpg/250px-Itsukushima_Gate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Itsukushima_Gate.jpg)

The *[torii](/wiki/Torii "Torii")* of [Itsukushima Shinto Shrine](/wiki/Itsukushima_Shinto_Shrine "Itsukushima Shinto Shrine") near [Hiroshima](/wiki/Hiroshima "Hiroshima")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/230128_Kamakura_Daibutsu_Japan04s3.jpg/250px-230128_Kamakura_Daibutsu_Japan04s3.jpg)](/wiki/File:230128_Kamakura_Daibutsu_Japan04s3.jpg)

The Great [Buddha](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha") at [Kōtoku-in](/wiki/K%C5%8Dtoku-in "Kōtoku-in") in [Kamakura](/wiki/Kamakura "Kamakura")

Japan's consti­tu­tion guaran­tees full religious freedom.[[264]](#cite_note-Inoue2007-265) Upper estimates suggest that 84–96% of the Japanese population subscribe to [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") as its indigenous religion.[[265]](#cite_note-266) However, these estimates are based on people [affiliated](/wiki/Danka_system "Danka system") with a temple, rather than the number of true believers. Many Japanese people practice both Shinto and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Japan "Buddhism in Japan"); they can identify with both religions or describe themselves as non-religious or spiritual.[[266]](#cite_note-267) The level of participation in religious ceremonies as a cultural tradition remains high, especially during [festivals](/wiki/Japanese_festivals "Japanese festivals") and occasions such as the [first shrine visit](/wiki/Hatsum%C5%8Dde "Hatsumōde") of the [New Year](/wiki/Japanese_New_Year "Japanese New Year").[[267]](#cite_note-268) [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism_in_Japan "Taoism in Japan") and [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism_in_Japan "Confucianism in Japan") from China have also influenced Japanese beliefs and customs.[[32]](#cite_note-Totman_2005-33)

In 2018, 1%[[268]](#cite_note-269) to 1.5% of the population were [Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Japan "Christianity in Japan").[[269]](#cite_note-270) Throughout the latest century, Western customs originally related to Christianity, including [Western style weddings](/wiki/Marriage_in_Japan "Marriage in Japan"), [Valentine's Day](/wiki/Valentine%27s_Day "Valentine's Day") and [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), have become popular as secular customs among many Japanese.[[270]](#cite_note-271)

About 90% of those practicing [Islam in Japan](/wiki/Islam_in_Japan "Islam in Japan") are foreign-born migrants as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[271]](#cite_note-272) In 2018, there were an estimated 105 [mosques](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") and 200,000 Muslims in Japan, 43,000 of which were Japanese nationals.[[272]](#cite_note-273) Other minority religions include [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Japan "Hinduism in Japan"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism_in_Japan "Judaism in Japan"), and [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith_in_Japan "Baháʼí Faith in Japan"), as well as the [animist](/wiki/Animist "Animist") beliefs of the Ainu.[[273]](#cite_note-274)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Japan](/wiki/Education_in_Japan "Education in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Tokyo_University_Entrance_Exam_Results_6.JPG/250px-Tokyo_University_Entrance_Exam_Results_6.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tokyo_University_Entrance_Exam_Results_6.JPG)

Students celebrating after the announcement of the results of the [entrance examinations](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Japan#University_entrance "Higher education in Japan") to the [University of Tokyo](/wiki/University_of_Tokyo "University of Tokyo")

Since the 1947 [Fundamental Law of Education](/wiki/Fundamental_Law_of_Education "Fundamental Law of Education"), compulsory education in Japan comprises [elementary](/wiki/Elementary_schools_in_Japan "Elementary schools in Japan") and [junior high school](/wiki/Secondary_education_in_Japan#Junior_high_school "Secondary education in Japan"), which together last for nine years.[[274]](#cite_note-275) Almost all children continue their education at a three-year [senior high school](/wiki/Secondary_education_in_Japan "Secondary education in Japan").[[275]](#cite_note-276) The top-ranking university in Japan is the [University of Tokyo](/wiki/University_of_Tokyo "University of Tokyo").[[276]](#cite_note-277) Starting in April 2016, various schools began the academic year with elementary school and junior high school integrated into one nine-year compulsory schooling program. [MEXT](/wiki/MEXT "MEXT") plans for this approach to be adopted nationwide.[[277]](#cite_note-278)

The [Programme for International Student Assessment](/wiki/Programme_for_International_Student_Assessment "Programme for International Student Assessment") (PISA) coordinated by the OECD ranks the knowledge and skills of Japanese 15-year-olds as the third best in the world.[[278]](#cite_note-OECD_Student_performance-279) Japan is one of the top-performing [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") countries in reading literacy, math, and sciences with the average student scoring 520 and has one of the world's highest-educated labor forces among OECD countries.[[279]](#cite_note-280)[[278]](#cite_note-OECD_Student_performance-279)[[280]](#cite_note-281) It spent 7.4% of its total GDP on education in 2021.[[281]](#cite_note-282)

In 2023, Japan ranked third for the percentage of 25- to 64-year-olds that have attained tertiary education, at 56%.[[282]](#cite_note-OECD_GPS_Education-283) Approximately 65.5% of Japanese aged 25 to 34 have some form of tertiary education qualification, with bachelor's degrees being held by 34.8% of Japanese aged 25 to 64, the second most in the OECD after [South Korea](/wiki/Education_in_South_Korea "Education in South Korea").[[282]](#cite_note-OECD_GPS_Education-283) Japanese women are more highly educated than the men: 59% of women possess a university degree, compared to 52% of men.[[283]](#cite_note-284)

### Health

Main articles: [Health in Japan](/wiki/Health_in_Japan "Health in Japan") and [Health care system in Japan](/wiki/Health_care_system_in_Japan "Health care system in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/University_of_Tokyo_Hospital.JPG/250px-University_of_Tokyo_Hospital.JPG)](/wiki/File:University_of_Tokyo_Hospital.JPG)

[University of Tokyo Hospital](/wiki/University_of_Tokyo_Hospital "University of Tokyo Hospital")

Health care in Japan is provided by national and local governments. Payment for personal medical services is offered through a universal health insurance system that provides relative equality of access, with fees set by a government committee. People without insurance through employers can participate in a national health insurance program administered by local governments.[[284]](#cite_note-Ikegami2014-285) Since 1973, all elderly persons have been covered by government-sponsored insurance.[[285]](#cite_note-286)

Japan spent 11.42% of its total GDP on healthcare in 2022.[[286]](#cite_note-287) In 2020, the overall life expectancy in Japan at birth was 85 years (82 years for men and 88 years for women),[[287]](#cite_note-288)[[288]](#cite_note-289) the [highest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_life_expectancy "List of countries by life expectancy") in the world;[[289]](#cite_note-290) while it had a very low [infant mortality rate](/wiki/Infant_mortality_rate "Infant mortality rate") (2 per 1,000 [live births](/wiki/Live_birth_(human) "Live birth (human)")).[[290]](#cite_note-291) Since 1981, the principal cause of death in Japan is [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer"), which accounted for 27% of the total deaths in 2018—followed by [cardiovascular diseases](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease"), which led to 15% of the deaths.[[291]](#cite_note-292) Japan has one of the world's [highest suicide rates](/wiki/Suicide_in_Japan "Suicide in Japan"), which is considered a major social issue.[[292]](#cite_note-293) Another significant public health issue is [smoking](/wiki/Smoking_in_Japan "Smoking in Japan") among Japanese men.[[293]](#cite_note-294) Japan has the lowest rate of heart disease in the OECD, and the lowest level of [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") among developed countries.[[294]](#cite_note-295)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Japan](/wiki/Culture_of_Japan "Culture of Japan")

See also: [Japanese popular culture](/wiki/Japanese_popular_culture "Japanese popular culture")

Contemporary Japanese culture combines influences from Asia, Europe, and North America.[[295]](#cite_note-Haffner-296) Traditional Japanese arts include [crafts](/wiki/Japanese_handicrafts "Japanese handicrafts") such as [ceramics](/wiki/Japanese_pottery_and_porcelain "Japanese pottery and porcelain"), [textiles](/wiki/Kimono "Kimono"), [lacquerware](/wiki/Japanese_lacquerware "Japanese lacquerware"), [swords](/wiki/Japanese_sword "Japanese sword"), and [dolls](/wiki/Japanese_traditional_dolls "Japanese traditional dolls"); performances of *[bunraku](/wiki/Bunraku "Bunraku")*, *[kabuki](/wiki/Kabuki "Kabuki")*, *[noh](/wiki/Noh "Noh")*, [dance](/wiki/Japanese_traditional_dance "Japanese traditional dance"), and *[rakugo](/wiki/Rakugo "Rakugo")*; and other practices, the [tea ceremony](/wiki/Japanese_tea_ceremony "Japanese tea ceremony"), *[ikebana](/wiki/Ikebana "Ikebana")*, [martial arts](/wiki/Japanese_martial_arts "Japanese martial arts"), [calligraphy](/wiki/Japanese_calligraphy "Japanese calligraphy"), [origami](/wiki/Origami "Origami"), *[onsen](/wiki/Onsen "Onsen")*, [Geisha](/wiki/Geisha "Geisha"), and [games](/wiki/List_of_Japanese_games "List of Japanese games"). Japan has a developed system for the protection and promotion of both tangible and intangible [Cultural Properties](/wiki/Cultural_Properties_of_Japan "Cultural Properties of Japan") and [National Treasures](/wiki/National_Treasures_of_Japan "National Treasures of Japan").[[296]](#cite_note-297) [Twenty-two sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Japan "List of World Heritage Sites in Japan") have been inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List, eighteen of which are of cultural significance.[[297]](#cite_note-unesco1-298) Japan is considered a [cultural superpower](/wiki/Cultural_superpower "Cultural superpower").[[298]](#cite_note-299)[[299]](#cite_note-300)[[300]](#cite_note-301)[[301]](#cite_note-302)

### Art and architecture

Main articles: [Japanese art](/wiki/Japanese_art "Japanese art") and [Japanese architecture](/wiki/Japanese_architecture "Japanese architecture")

Further information: [Japanese garden](/wiki/Japanese_garden "Japanese garden"), [Japanese esthetics](/wiki/Japanese_esthetics "Japanese esthetics"), [Japanese painting](/wiki/Japanese_painting "Japanese painting"), and [Japanese sculpture](/wiki/Japanese_sculpture "Japanese sculpture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/The_Great_Wave_off_Kanagawa.jpg/250px-The_Great_Wave_off_Kanagawa.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Great_Wave_off_Kanagawa.jpg)

[Hokusai](/wiki/Hokusai "Hokusai")'s 19th-century [ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") [woodblock print](/wiki/Woodblock_printing_in_Japan "Woodblock printing in Japan") *[The Great Wave off Kanagawa](/wiki/The_Great_Wave_off_Kanagawa "The Great Wave off Kanagawa")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/150504_Ritsurin_Park_Takamatsu_Kagawa_pref_Japan01s3.jpg/250px-150504_Ritsurin_Park_Takamatsu_Kagawa_pref_Japan01s3.jpg)](/wiki/File:150504_Ritsurin_Park_Takamatsu_Kagawa_pref_Japan01s3.jpg)

[Ritsurin Garden](/wiki/Ritsurin_Garden "Ritsurin Garden"), one of the most famous strolling gardens in Japan

The history of Japanese painting exhibits synthesis and competition between native Japanese esthetics and imported ideas.[[302]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-303) The interaction between Japanese and European art has been significant: for example [ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") prints, which began to be exported in the 19th century in the movement known as [Japonism](/wiki/Japonism "Japonism"), had a significant influence on the development of modern art in the West, most notably on [post-Impressionism](/wiki/Post-Impressionism "Post-Impressionism").[[302]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-303)

Japanese architecture is a combination of local and other influences. It has traditionally been typified by wooden or mud plaster structures, elevated slightly off the ground, with tiled or thatched roofs.[[303]](#cite_note-304) [Traditional housing](/wiki/Minka "Minka") and many [temple buildings](/wiki/Japanese_Buddhist_architecture "Japanese Buddhist architecture") see the use of [tatami](/wiki/Tatami "Tatami") mats and [sliding doors](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dji "Shōji") that break down the distinction between rooms and indoor and outdoor space.[[304]](#cite_note-305) Since the 19th century, Japan has incorporated much of Western [modern architecture](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture") into construction and design.[[305]](#cite_note-306) It was not until after World War II that Japanese architects made an impression on the international scene, firstly with the work of architects like [Kenzō Tange](/wiki/Kenz%C5%8D_Tange "Kenzō Tange") and then with movements like [Metabolism](/wiki/Metabolist_Movement "Metabolist Movement").[[306]](#cite_note-307)

### Literature and philosophy

Main articles: [Japanese literature](/wiki/Japanese_literature "Japanese literature") and [Japanese philosophy](/wiki/Japanese_philosophy "Japanese philosophy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Genji_emaki_01003_001.jpg/250px-Genji_emaki_01003_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Genji_emaki_01003_001.jpg)

12th-century [illustrated handscroll](/wiki/Genji_Monogatari_Emaki "Genji Monogatari Emaki") of *[The Tale of Genji](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Genji "The Tale of Genji")*, a [National Treasure](/wiki/National_Treasures_of_Japan "National Treasures of Japan")

The earliest works of Japanese literature include the *[Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki")* and *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")* chronicles and the *[Man'yōshū](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Man'yōshū")* [poetry anthology](/wiki/List_of_Japanese_poetry_anthologies "List of Japanese poetry anthologies"), all from the 8th century and written in Chinese characters.[[307]](#cite_note-308)[[308]](#cite_note-309) In the early Heian period, the system of [phonograms](/wiki/Phonogram_(linguistics) "Phonogram (linguistics)") known as kana ([hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana") and [katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana")) was developed.[[309]](#cite_note-310) *[The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter](/wiki/The_Tale_of_the_Bamboo_Cutter "The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter")* is considered the oldest extant Japanese narrative.[[310]](#cite_note-311) An account of court life is given in *[The Pillow Book](/wiki/The_Pillow_Book "The Pillow Book")* by [Sei Shōnagon](/wiki/Sei_Sh%C5%8Dnagon "Sei Shōnagon"), while *[The Tale of Genji](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Genji "The Tale of Genji")* by [Murasaki Shikibu](/wiki/Murasaki_Shikibu "Murasaki Shikibu") is often described as the world's first novel.[[311]](#cite_note-312)[[312]](#cite_note-313)

During the Edo period, the *[chōnin](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dnin "Chōnin")* ('townspeople') overtook the samurai aristocracy as producers and consumers of literature. The popularity of the works of [Saikaku](/wiki/Saikaku "Saikaku"), for example, reveals this change in readership and authorship, while [Bashō](/wiki/Bash%C5%8D "Bashō") revivified the poetic tradition of the *[Kokinshū](/wiki/Kokinsh%C5%AB "Kokinshū")* with his *[haikai](/wiki/Haikai "Haikai")* ([haiku](/wiki/Haiku "Haiku")) and wrote the poetic travelogue *[Oku no Hosomichi](/wiki/Oku_no_Hosomichi "Oku no Hosomichi")*.[[313]](#cite_note-314) The Meiji era saw the decline of traditional literary forms as Japanese literature integrated Western influences. [Natsume Sōseki](/wiki/Natsume_S%C5%8Dseki "Natsume Sōseki") and [Mori Ōgai](/wiki/Mori_%C5%8Cgai "Mori Ōgai") were significant novelists in the early 20th century, followed by [Ryūnosuke Akutagawa](/wiki/Ry%C5%ABnosuke_Akutagawa "Ryūnosuke Akutagawa"), [Jun'ichirō Tanizaki](/wiki/Jun%27ichir%C5%8D_Tanizaki "Jun'ichirō Tanizaki"), [Kafū Nagai](/wiki/Kaf%C5%AB_Nagai "Kafū Nagai") and, more recently, [Haruki Murakami](/wiki/Haruki_Murakami "Haruki Murakami") and [Kenji Nakagami](/wiki/Kenji_Nakagami "Kenji Nakagami"). Japan has two [Nobel Prize–winning authors](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature")—[Yasunari Kawabata](/wiki/Yasunari_Kawabata "Yasunari Kawabata") (1968) and [Kenzaburō Ōe](/wiki/Kenzabur%C5%8D_%C5%8Ce "Kenzaburō Ōe") (1994).[[314]](#cite_note-315)

Japanese philosophy has historically been a [fusion](/wiki/Information_fusion "Information fusion") of both foreign, particularly [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_philosophy "Chinese philosophy") and [Western](/wiki/Western_philosophy "Western philosophy"), and uniquely Japanese elements. In its literary forms, Japanese philosophy began about fourteen centuries ago. Confucian ideals remain evident in the [Japanese concept of society](/wiki/Japanese_society "Japanese society") and the self, and in the organization of the government and the structure of society.[[315]](#cite_note-Japanese_Confucian_Philosophy-316) Buddhism has profoundly impacted Japanese psychology, metaphysics, and esthetics.[[316]](#cite_note-317)

### Performing arts

Main articles: [Music of Japan](/wiki/Music_of_Japan "Music of Japan") and [Theatre of Japan](/wiki/Theatre_of_Japan "Theatre of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/%E6%98%A5%E6%97%A5%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BC%E7%AF%A0%E5%B1%B1%E3%83%BC%E7%BF%81%E5%A5%89%E7%B4%8DP1011774.jpg/250px-%E6%98%A5%E6%97%A5%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BC%E7%AF%A0%E5%B1%B1%E3%83%BC%E7%BF%81%E5%A5%89%E7%B4%8DP1011774.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%98%A5%E6%97%A5%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE%E3%83%BC%E7%AF%A0%E5%B1%B1%E3%83%BC%E7%BF%81%E5%A5%89%E7%B4%8DP1011774.jpg)

*[Noh](/wiki/Noh "Noh")* performance at a Shinto shrine

Japanese music is eclectic and diverse. Many [instruments](/wiki/Traditional_Japanese_musical_instruments "Traditional Japanese musical instruments"), such as the [koto](/wiki/Koto_(instrument) "Koto (instrument)"), were introduced in the 9th and 10th centuries. The popular [folk music](/wiki/Music_of_Japan#Folk_music "Music of Japan"), with the guitar-like *[shamisen](/wiki/Shamisen "Shamisen")*, dates from the 16th century.[[317]](#cite_note-318) Western classical music, introduced in the late 19th century, forms an integral part of Japanese culture.[[318]](#cite_note-319) *[Kumi-daiko](/wiki/Kumi-daiko "Kumi-daiko")* (ensemble drumming) was developed in post-war Japan and became very popular in North America.[[319]](#cite_note-320) Popular music in post-war Japan has been heavily influenced by American and European trends, which has led to the evolution of [J-pop](/wiki/J-pop "J-pop").[[320]](#cite_note-321) [Karaoke](/wiki/Karaoke "Karaoke") is a significant cultural activity.[[321]](#cite_note-322)

The four traditional theaters from Japan are *[noh](/wiki/Noh "Noh")*, *[kyōgen](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dgen "Kyōgen")*, *[kabuki](/wiki/Kabuki "Kabuki")*, and *[bunraku](/wiki/Bunraku "Bunraku")*.[[322]](#cite_note-323) *Noh* is one of the oldest continuous theater traditions in the world.[[323]](#cite_note-324)

### Media

Main article: [Mass media in Japan](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Japan "Mass media in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Pokemon_Center_Tohoku.JPG/250px-Pokemon_Center_Tohoku.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pokemon_Center_Tohoku.JPG)

The Japanese media franchise *[Pokémon](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon "Pokémon")* is the [highest grossing media franchise](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_media_franchises "List of highest-grossing media franchises") of all time.

According to the 2015 NHK survey on television viewing in Japan, 79% of Japanese watch television daily.[[324]](#cite_note-325) [Japanese television dramas](/wiki/Japanese_television_drama "Japanese television drama") are viewed both within Japan and internationally.[[325]](#cite_note-326) Many Japanese [media franchises](/wiki/Media_franchise "Media franchise") have gained considerable global popularity and are among the world's [highest-grossing media franchises](/wiki/Highest-grossing_media_franchises "Highest-grossing media franchises"). [Japanese newspapers](/wiki/Japanese_newspapers "Japanese newspapers") are among the most circulated in the world as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Japan&action=edit).[[326]](#cite_note-327)

Japan has one of the oldest and largest film industries globally.[[327]](#cite_note-328) [Ishirō Honda](/wiki/Ishir%C5%8D_Honda "Ishirō Honda")'s *[Godzilla](/wiki/Godzilla_(1954_film) "Godzilla (1954 film)")* became an international icon of Japan and spawned an entire subgenre of [kaiju](/wiki/Kaiju "Kaiju") films, as well as the longest-running film franchise in history.[[328]](#cite_note-329)[[329]](#cite_note-330) Japanese comics, known as [manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga"), developed in the mid-20th century and have become popular [worldwide](/wiki/Manga_outside_Japan "Manga outside Japan").[[330]](#cite_note-Kinko_Ito_456–475-331)[[331]](#cite_note-332) A large number of [manga series](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_manga "List of best-selling manga") have become some of the [best-selling comics series](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_comic_series "List of best-selling comic series") of all time, rivalling the [American comics industry](/wiki/American_comic_book "American comic book").[[332]](#cite_note-333) Japanese animated films and television series, known as [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime"), were largely influenced by Japanese manga and have become highly popular globally.[[333]](#cite_note-334)[[334]](#cite_note-335)

### Holidays

Main article: [Public holidays in Japan](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Japan "Public holidays in Japan")

See also: [Japanese festivals](/wiki/Japanese_festivals "Japanese festivals")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Young_ladies_at_Harajuku.jpg/250px-Young_ladies_at_Harajuku.jpg)](/wiki/File:Young_ladies_at_Harajuku.jpg)

Young women celebrate [Coming of Age Day](/wiki/Coming_of_Age_Day "Coming of Age Day") (成人の日, *Seijin no Hi*) in [Harajuku](/wiki/Harajuku "Harajuku"), Tokyo

Officially, Japan has 16 national, government-recognized holidays. Public holidays in Japan are regulated by the Public Holiday Law (国民の祝日に関する法律, *Kokumin no Shukujitsu ni Kansuru Hōritsu*) of 1948.[[335]](#cite_note-336) Beginning in 2000, Japan implemented the [Happy Monday System](/wiki/Happy_Monday_System "Happy Monday System"), which moved a number of national holidays to Monday in order to obtain a long weekend.[[336]](#cite_note-337) The national holidays in Japan are [New Year's Day](/wiki/Japanese_New_Year "Japanese New Year") on January 1, [Coming of Age Day](/wiki/Coming_of_Age_Day "Coming of Age Day") on the second Monday of January, [National Foundation Day](/wiki/National_Foundation_Day_(Japan) "National Foundation Day (Japan)") on February 11, [The Emperor's Birthday](/wiki/The_Emperor%27s_Birthday "The Emperor's Birthday") on February 23, [Vernal Equinox Day](/wiki/Vernal_Equinox_Day "Vernal Equinox Day") on March 20 or 21, [Shōwa Day](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dwa_Day "Shōwa Day") on April 29, [Constitution Memorial Day](/wiki/Constitution_Memorial_Day "Constitution Memorial Day") on May 3, [Greenery Day](/wiki/Greenery_Day "Greenery Day") on May 4, [Children's Day](/wiki/Children%27s_Day_(Japan) "Children's Day (Japan)") on May 5, [Marine Day](/wiki/Marine_Day "Marine Day") on the third Monday of July, [Mountain Day](/wiki/Mountain_Day "Mountain Day") on August 11, [Respect for the Aged Day](/wiki/Respect_for_the_Aged_Day "Respect for the Aged Day") on the third Monday of September, [Autumnal Equinox](/wiki/September_equinox "September equinox") on September 23 or 24, [Health and Sports Day](/wiki/Health_and_Sports_Day "Health and Sports Day") on the second Monday of October, [Culture Day](/wiki/Culture_Day "Culture Day") on November 3, and [Labor Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/Labor_Thanksgiving_Day "Labor Thanksgiving Day") on November 23.[[337]](#cite_note-338)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Japanese cuisine](/wiki/Japanese_cuisine "Japanese cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/East_West_sushi_01.jpg/250px-East_West_sushi_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:East_West_sushi_01.jpg)

A plate of *[nigirizushi](/wiki/Nigirizushi "Nigirizushi")*

Japanese cuisine offers a vast array of [regional specialties](/wiki/Japanese_regional_cuisine "Japanese regional cuisine") that use traditional recipes and local ingredients.[[338]](#cite_note-339) Seafood and [Japanese rice](/wiki/Japanese_rice "Japanese rice") or [noodles](/wiki/Japanese_noodles "Japanese noodles") are traditional staples.[[339]](#cite_note-340) [Japanese curry](/wiki/Japanese_curry "Japanese curry"), since its introduction to Japan from [British India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), is so widely consumed that it can be termed a [national dish](/wiki/National_dish "National dish"), alongside [ramen](/wiki/Ramen "Ramen") and [sushi](/wiki/Sushi "Sushi").[[340]](#cite_note-341)[[341]](#cite_note-342) Traditional Japanese sweets are known as *wagashi*.[[342]](#cite_note-Goldstein2015-343) Ingredients such as [red bean paste](/wiki/Red_bean_paste "Red bean paste") and [mochi](/wiki/Mochi "Mochi") are used. More modern-day tastes include [green tea ice cream](/wiki/Green_tea_ice_cream "Green tea ice cream").[[343]](#cite_note-FujitaStallings2008-344)

Popular Japanese beverages include [sake](/wiki/Sake "Sake"), a brewed rice beverage that typically contains 14–17% alcohol and is made by multiple fermentation of rice.[[344]](#cite_note-Batt2014-345) Beer has been brewed in Japan since the late 17th century.[[345]](#cite_note-BoultonQuain2013-346) [Green tea](/wiki/Green_tea "Green tea") is produced in Japan and prepared in forms such as [matcha](/wiki/Matcha "Matcha"), used in the [Japanese tea ceremony](/wiki/Japanese_tea_ceremony "Japanese tea ceremony").[[346]](#cite_note-347)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Japan](/wiki/Sport_in_Japan "Sport in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Aki_basho_dohy%C5%8D-iri_on_Sept._28_2014.jpg/250px-Aki_basho_dohy%C5%8D-iri_on_Sept._28_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aki_basho_dohy%C5%8D-iri_on_Sept._28_2014.jpg)

[Sumo](/wiki/Sumo "Sumo") wrestlers form around the referee during the ring-entering ceremony.

Traditionally, [sumo](/wiki/Sumo "Sumo") is considered Japan's national sport.[[347]](#cite_note-348) Japanese martial arts such as [judo](/wiki/Judo "Judo") and [kendo](/wiki/Kendo "Kendo") are taught as part of the compulsory junior high school curriculum.[[348]](#cite_note-349) [Karate](/wiki/Karate "Karate"), which originated in the Ryukyu Kingdom, is popular across the world and has been [included in the Olympic Games](/wiki/Karate_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Karate at the Summer Olympics").[[349]](#cite_note-350)[[350]](#cite_note-351)[[351]](#cite_note-352) [Baseball](/wiki/Baseball_in_Japan "Baseball in Japan") is the most popular sport in the country.[[352]](#cite_note-353) Japan's top professional league, [Nippon Professional Baseball](/wiki/Nippon_Professional_Baseball "Nippon Professional Baseball") (NPB), was established in 1936.[[353]](#cite_note-354) Since the establishment of the [Japan Professional Football League](/wiki/Japan_Professional_Football_League "Japan Professional Football League") (J.League) in 1992, association football gained a wide following.[[354]](#cite_note-355) The country co-hosted the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") with South Korea.[[355]](#cite_note-356) Japan has one of the most successful football teams in Asia, winning the [Asian Cup](/wiki/Asian_Cup "Asian Cup") four times,[[356]](#cite_note-357) and the [FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/2011_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "2011 FIFA Women's World Cup") in 2011.[[357]](#cite_note-358) Golf is also popular in Japan.[[358]](#cite_note-359)

In [motorsport](/wiki/Motorsport "Motorsport"), Japanese automotive manufacturers have been successful in multiple different categories, with titles and victories in series such as [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One"), [MotoGP](/wiki/Grand_Prix_motorcycle_racing "Grand Prix motorcycle racing"), and the [World Rally Championship](/wiki/World_Rally_Championship "World Rally Championship").[[359]](#cite_note-360)[[360]](#cite_note-361)[[361]](#cite_note-362) Drivers from Japan have victories at the [Indianapolis 500](/wiki/Indianapolis_500 "Indianapolis 500") and the [24 Hours of Le Mans](/wiki/24_Hours_of_Le_Mans "24 Hours of Le Mans") as well as podium finishes in Formula One, in addition to success in domestic championships.[[362]](#cite_note-363)[[363]](#cite_note-364) [Super GT](/wiki/Super_GT "Super GT") is the most popular national racing series in Japan, while [Super Formula](/wiki/Super_Formula "Super Formula") is the top-level domestic open-wheel series.[[364]](#cite_note-365) The country hosts major races such as the [Japanese Grand Prix](/wiki/Japanese_Grand_Prix "Japanese Grand Prix").[[365]](#cite_note-366)

Japan hosted the Summer Olympics in [Tokyo in 1964](/wiki/1964_Summer_Olympics "1964 Summer Olympics") and the Winter Olympics in [Sapporo in 1972](/wiki/1972_Winter_Olympics "1972 Winter Olympics") and [Nagano in 1998](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics").[[366]](#cite_note-367) The country hosted the official [2006 Basketball World Championship](/wiki/2006_Basketball_World_Championship "2006 Basketball World Championship")[[367]](#cite_note-368) and co-hosted the [2023 Basketball World Championship](/wiki/2023_Basketball_World_Championship "2023 Basketball World Championship").[[368]](#cite_note-369) Tokyo hosted the [2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics") in 2021, making Tokyo the first Asian city to host the Olympics twice.[[369]](#cite_note-370) The country gained the hosting rights for the official [Women's Volleyball World Championship](/wiki/Women%27s_Volleyball_World_Championship "Women's Volleyball World Championship") on five occasions, more than any other country.[[370]](#cite_note-371) Japan is the most successful Asian [Rugby Union](/wiki/Rugby_Union "Rugby Union") country[[371]](#cite_note-372) and hosted the 2019 IRB [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup").[[372]](#cite_note-373)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** 日本 **Nihon** [[ɲihoꜜɴ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ja-nihon(%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC).ogg "File:Ja-nihon(日本).ogg") or *Nippon* [[ɲippoꜜɴ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ja-nippon(%E6%97%A5%E6%9C%AC).ogg "File:Ja-nippon(日本).ogg"), formally 日本国, **Nihon-koku** or **Nippon-koku**. In Japanese, the name of the country as it appears on official documents, including [the country's constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Japan "Constitution of Japan"), is 日本国, meaning 'State of Japan'. The short name 日本 is also often used officially. In English, the official name of the country is simply "Japan".[[10]](#cite_note-10)
